1: Assessing the environmental impact of resource recovery from dairy manure
Callan J. Glover Alyssa McDonnell b , Kimberly S. Rollins b , Sage R. Hiibel a , Pablo K. Cornejo c,*

<list>a Department of Chemical and Materials Engineering, University of Nevada, Reno, Reno, NV, USA 
b Department of Agricultural and Resource Economics, University of Connecticut, Storrs, CT, USA 
C Civil Engineering Department, California State University, Chico, Chico, CA, USA</list>

ARTICLEINFO|ABSTRACT Keywords: Manure Life cycle assessment Carbon trading|Manure management is a major contributor to environmental impacts from large-scale dairy production. In this study, technologies for recovering energy, nutrients, and water from dairy manure were evaluated using life cycle assessment (LCA) and compared to conventional practices on California dairy farms. Six scenarios were evaluated: conventional manure management practices, anaerobic digestion (AD) for biogas recovery, and four scenarios for nutrients, energy, and water integrated recovery, called NEWIR. The NEWIR system consists of hydrothermal carbonization (HTC) for energy recovery via hydrochar, algae cultivation in the HTC aqueous product for nutrient recovery and production of protein-rich cattle feed, and water recovery from algae pond effluent via membrane distillation. Four NEWIR scenarios were evaluated, each with a different species of algae. Based on the results of the LCA, AD improves GHG emissions relative to conventional practices by 82%, but has similar eutrophication impacts, posing similar concerns for nutrient management as current practices. Results for the NEWIR system are highly dependent on the algae species used. Three of the four species evaluated (Chla- mydomonas reinhardtii, Chlorella vulgaris, and Scenedesmus obliquus) improve GHG emissions by 420-500 kg CO2- eq. per functional unit, while net water consumption is increased by approximately 75% over AD and conven- tional practices Spirulina maxima requires more water and chemical inputs for cultivation than the other species, resulting in higher water use (21 times higher than baseline), though GHG emissions are still reduced by 85 kg CO2-eq. per functional unit relative to conventional practices. All NEWIR scenarios improve eutrophication impacts relative to AD and conventional practices by 16-46% for marine eutrophication and 18-99% for freshwater eutrophication, depending on the algae species used. The results suggest integrated resource recovery through NEWIR is a promising treatment method for manure to mitigate GHG emissions and improve nutrient management on large-scale farms. In addition, carbon and nutrient trading policies are discussed in relation to resource recovery technologies and their potential to incentivize producers to recover products from dairy manure.
2: The sustainability of livestock production has been a growing concern in recent years (Steinfeld et al., 2007). The dairy industry is no exception, with increasing scrutiny of greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions associated with milk production, as well as water consumption and water quality degradation. A 2021 study of the US dairy industry found that milk production is responsible for around 1.5% of the total US GHG emissions (Rotz e et al., 2021). Manure management is one of the primary contributors to environmental impacts, particularly through GHG

emissions and nutrient contamination of water resources. In 2020, an estimated 53% of manure methane emissions came from dairy manure (U.S. EPA, 2022). Consolidation of farms into larger operations has led to challenges in managing nitrogen (N) and phosphorus (P), potentially exceeding crop requirements on surrounding land and causing water quality problems such as surface eutrophication and groundwater contamination (EPA, 2013). Given concerns for climate change and water quality issues related to manure management, further research is needed to assess alternative manure management strategies.
Recent focus has been on resource recovery from waste streams,

including manure (Mayer et al., 2019). Manure is a rich source of organic matter and nutrients with potential for bioenergy generation and nutrient capture. Resource recovery includes benefits from pro- ducing a useful product from manure, as well as potentially reducing environmental impacts from conventional management. One emerging technology for energy recovery from wet waste streams such as manure is hydrothermal carbonization (HTC). HTC is a thermochemical process using high temperature (180-250°C) and pressure to carbonize manure, producing a solid fuel called hydrochar and a liquid aqueous product (Lachos-Perez et al., 2022). Hydrochar has similar properties to lignite coal (Reza et al., 2016) and can be combusted in coal-fired power plants to produce heat and power (Reza et al., 2014), while avoiding upstream processes needed for raw material extraction. HTC has been shown to improve GHG emissions for energy production relative to coal through life cycle assessment (LCA) (Berge et al., 2015; Glover et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2021), making it a promising technology for waste-to-energy production. However, the HTC aqueous product (HAP), typically handled as a waste stream, has potential for negative envi- ronmental consequences due to nutrients remaining in solution (Bena- vente et al., 2017; Berge et al., 2015). Additional treatment of the HAP is needed to improve environmental impacts associated with this technology.

Because HAP contains a portion of the manure nutrients (Reza et al., 2016), HTC may be combined with a subsequent nutrient recovery step (Aragón-Briceño et al., 2021). One option is algae cultivation, which has been widely studied in a variety of wastewater sources, including dairy manure (Choi et al., 2018; Lv et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2010) and hy- drothermal effluents (Belete et al., 2019; Du et al., 2012; Yao et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2016). High nutrient removal rates have been ach- ieved with algae in both raw and digested dairy manure, ranging from 60 to 90% or greater of N and 70-100% of P depending on the species (Pang et al., 2020; Mulbry et al., 2008). Nutrient recovery from hydro- thermal effluents is more variable (Belete et al., 2019; Du et al., 2012), and HAP typically needs to be diluted to support growth, potentially due to the presence of inhibiting organic compounds (Zhang et al., 2016). Algae remove nutrients from solution, reducing possible eutrophication impact prior to discharge (Higgins and Kendall, 2012; Wu et al., 2020). Additionally, algae can be used as a protein-rich animal feed (Madeira et al., 2017) in place of conventional crops.

In this study, a system for nutrients, energy, and water integrated recovery (NEWIR), is evaluated with LCA to determine the environ- mental impacts associated with resource recovery from manure (Glover et al., 2022). The system consists of HTC for energy recovery through hydrochar, algae cultivation for nutrient recovery, and membrane distillation (MD) for water recovery. MD is a thermally-driven mem- brane separation process that can utilize waste heat (Gustafson et al., 2018), reducing energy requirements compared to other membrane methods like reverse osmosis (RO). The NEWIR system is intended to address multiple areas of environmental impact from dairy manure, while recovering valuable resources. In a previous study, NEWIR was compared to conventional manure management practices. Glover et al. (2022) found that NEWIR can improve GHG and nutrient emissions, but drastically increases water consumption to support algae growth (Glover et al., 2022). A key contributor to impacts was cultivation of algae species Spirulina maxima due to chemical additions required for culti- vation. Consequently, additional research has evaluated the growth of three other algae species in HAP with potential to reduce water and chemical consumption. The current study includes all four algae species to assess how varying algae species affects LCA results.

In addition to NEWIR, an LCA is performed on conventional manure management practices and an existing energy recovery technology, anaerobic digestion (AD) in California (CA). CA is the top dairy- producing state in the US with 19% of the US supply and over 1.7 million dairy cows (CDFA, 2020). Manure management is a major contributor to both GHG emissions and nitrate contamination of water resources in the state (CARB, 2019; Rosenstock et al., 2014). AD is

encouraged as an alternative manure treatment method to capture methane and meet emission reduction targets (CARB, 2022a; Senate Bill 1383, 2016). However, AD has not been compared to HTC or an inte- grated resource recovery method such as NEWIR to evaluate environ- mental impacts in previous studies.

The purpose of this study is to compare the environmental impacts of resource recovery through NEWIR to existing manure management strategies and AD. Specific objectives are to 1) develop life cycle in- ventory data for the NEWIR system using four algae species, 2) develop life cycle inventory data for AD treatment of dairy manure based on current CA practices, 3) evaluate environmental impacts relative to conventional manure management to assess benefits and possible tradeoffs of resource recovery strategies, and 4) discuss carbon and nutrient policy perspectives in the context of resource recovery from dairy manure.

Given concerns for climate change and water quality issues related to manure management, further research is needed to assess alternative manure management strategies.
Recent focus has been on resource recovery from waste streams,

including manure (Mayer et al., 2019). Manure is a rich source of organic matter and nutrients with potential for bioenergy generation and nutrient capture. Resource recovery includes benefits from pro- ducing a useful product from manure, as well as potentially reducing environmental impacts from conventional management. One emerging technology for energy recovery from wet waste streams such as manure is hydrothermal carbonization (HTC). HTC is a thermochemical process using high temperature (180-250°C) and pressure to carbonize manure, producing a solid fuel called hydrochar and a liquid aqueous product (Lachos-Perez et al., 2022). Hydrochar has similar properties to lignite coal (Reza et al., 2016) and can be combusted in coal-fired power plants to produce heat and power (Reza et al., 2014), while avoiding upstream processes needed for raw material extraction. HTC has been shown to improve GHG emissions for energy production relative to coal through life cycle assessment (LCA) (Berge et al., 2015; Glover et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2021), making it a promising technology for waste-to-energy production. However, the HTC aqueous product (HAP), typically handled as a waste stream, has potential for negative envi- ronmental consequences due to nutrients remaining in solution (Bena- vente et al., 2017; Berge et al., 2015). Additional treatment of the HAP is needed to improve environmental impacts associated with this technology.

Because HAP contains a portion of the manure nutrients (Reza et al., 2016), HTC may be combined with a subsequent nutrient recovery step (Aragón-Briceño et al., 2021). One option is algae cultivation, which has been widely studied in a variety of wastewater sources, including dairy manure (Choi et al., 2018; Lv et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2010) and hy- drothermal effluents (Belete et al., 2019; Du et al., 2012; Yao et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2016). High nutrient removal rates have been ach- ieved with algae in both raw and digested dairy manure, ranging from 60 to 90% or greater of N and 70-100% of P depending on the species (Pang et al., 2020; Mulbry et al., 2008). Nutrient recovery from hydro- thermal effluents is more variable (Belete et al., 2019; Du et al., 2012), and HAP typically needs to be diluted to support growth, potentially due to the presence of inhibiting organic compounds (Zhang et al., 2016). Algae remove nutrients from solution, reducing possible eutrophication impact prior to discharge (Higgins and Kendall, 2012; Wu et al., 2020). Additionally, algae can be used as a protein-rich animal feed (Madeira et al., 2017) in place of conventional crops.

In this study, a system for nutrients, energy, and water integrated recovery (NEWIR), is evaluated with LCA to determine the environ- mental impacts associated with resource recovery from manure (Glover et al., 2022). The system consists of HTC for energy recovery through hydrochar, algae cultivation for nutrient recovery, and membrane distillation (MD) for water recovery. MD is a thermally-driven mem- brane separation process that can utilize waste heat (Gustafson et al., 2018), reducing energy requirements compared to other membrane methods like reverse osmosis (RO). The NEWIR system is intended to address multiple areas of environmental impact from dairy manure, while recovering valuable resources. In a previous study, NEWIR was compared to conventional manure management practices. Glover et al. (2022) found that NEWIR can improve GHG and nutrient emissions, but drastically increases water consumption to support algae growth (Glover et al., 2022). A key contributor to impacts was cultivation of algae species Spirulina maxima due to chemical additions required for culti- vation. Consequently, additional research has evaluated the growth of three other algae species in HAP with potential to reduce water and chemical consumption. The current study includes all four algae species to assess how varying algae species affects LCA results.

In addition to NEWIR, an LCA is performed on conventional manure management practices and an existing energy recovery technology, anaerobic digestion (AD) in California (CA). CA is the top dairy- producing state in the US with 19% of the US supply and over 1.7 million dairy cows (CDFA, 2020). Manure management is a major contributor to both GHG emissions and nitrate contamination of water resources in the state (CARB, 2019; Rosenstock et al., 2014). AD is

encouraged as an alternative manure treatment method to capture methane and meet emission reduction targets (CARB, 2022a; Senate Bill 1383, 2016). However, AD has not been compared to HTC or an inte- grated resource recovery method such as NEWIR to evaluate environ- mental impacts in previous studies.

The purpose of this study is to compare the environmental impacts of resource recovery through NEWIR to existing manure management strategies and AD. Specific objectives are to 1) develop life cycle in- ventory data for the NEWIR system using four algae species, 2) develop life cycle inventory data for AD treatment of dairy manure based on current CA practices, 3) evaluate environmental impacts relative to conventional manure management to assess benefits and possible tradeoffs of resource recovery strategies, and 4) discuss carbon and nutrient policy perspectives in the context of resource recovery from dairy manure.
8: The goal of the study is to compare environmental impacts of the NEWIR resource recovery system to impacts from conventional manure management practices and AD. Six scenarios are compared with LCA: conventional farm practices ("baseline"), anaerobic digestion ("AD"), and four NEWIR scenarios each involving a unique algae species. NEWIR-max uses Spirulina maxima, NEWIR-rein uses Chlamydomonas reinhardtii, NEWIR-vulg uses Chlorella vulgaris, and NEWIR-oblig uses Scenedesmus obliquus.

The LCA was performed in accordance with ISO standards (ISO, 2006). First, the goal and scope of the LCA study were defined. The functional unit of the study was 1000 kg raw manure entering each treatment system to capture the impacts associated with treating the same quantity of manure. In Section 2.3, the life cycle inventory (LCI) consisting of all inputs and outputs was developed for each scenario. Results were calculated using the ReCiPe life cycle impact assessment method as described in Section 2.4. Finally, the results were interpreted and evaluated using sensitivity and uncertainty analyses. The results of the study are intended to provide information about the environmental impacts of existing dairy manure management practices relative to new technologies for dairy producers, researchers, and other stakeholders.

10: The system boundaries in each scenario include all relevant pro- cesses for handling dairy manure from its generation in cow housing to its end use as a nutrient source on crop fields or as a recovered product. Farm processes that are not related to manure management, such as milk collection and processing, animal husbandry, and most feed production were assumed to be the same in all scenarios and therefore were excluded from the LCA. An exception is the production of soybean feed, which is included as an avoided product in the NEWIR scenarios to compare with the production of algae-based cattle feed.
11: The baseline scenario is intended to capture typical manure man- agement practices for large-scale dairies in CA. The system begins with manure collection on the farm and includes storage and land application of manure (Fig. 1). The farm uses freestall barns to house lactating dairy cows, a common practice on CA dairies (Meyer et al., 2011), with access to open corrals dependent on weather conditions. Manure is collected from the freestall barns and the milk parlor with a flush system and stored as a liquid in an uncovered anaerobic lagoon. Recycled lagoon water is used to flush the freestall barns (Meyer et al., 2011) while fresh water is used to flush the milk parlor areas (Summers and Williams, 2013). Solid manure is collected via scraping from the open corral and transfer lanes and stored in a solid pile. The proportion of manure
Fig. 1. System boundaries for the baseline scenario, which covers typical manure management practices on a large-scale freestall dairy in California.

collected as a liquid versus solid depends on the amount of time the cows spend in each location. At a freestall dairy, the majority of manure is typically collected by flushing (Meyer et al., 2019). In this study, it was assumed that 75% of the manure generated by the herd was collected by flushing and stored as a liquid, and 25% was collected and stored as a solid.

Both liquid and solid manure are used as nutrient sources to grow crops for the herd. In this study, manure nutrients are assumed to be applied to corn and wheat in the summer and winter growing seasons, respectively, to provide a portion of the dairy's forage crops (Miller et al., 2017). Lagoon water is applied to fields throughout the year through the irrigation system, while solids are incorporated into the soil between growing seasons. Application of manure nutrients to crop fields was based on regulations set by the 2013 Dairy Order that allows dairies to apply a maximum of 1.4 times the N harvested in the crop to fields receiving manure (R5-2013-0122, 2013). The applied N may come from multiple sources, including manure, synthetic fertilizer, or irrigation water containing nitrates. Synthetic fertilizer can be a significant contributor to groundwater contamination (Rosenstock et al., 2014), but its application varies significantly among dairies, even for the same crop type (Parsons, 2018). Because of the uncertainty involved in application of synthetic fertilizer, it was assumed in this study that manure was the

sole nutrient source for the crops, so the amount of land cultivated as corn and wheat was based on the available manure N applied at the limit set by the Dairy Order (R5-2013-0122, 2013). In practice, dairy pro- ducers may use nutrients from a combination of sources based on crop needs, timing of application in the crop growth cycle, and nutrient levels present in soil.

The AD scenario is intended to capture typical manure management practices on CA dairy farms that process manure in a covered lagoon digester and use recovered biogas to generate electricity onsite. AD captures methane that would otherwise be released to the atmosphere during anaerobic storage of manure. AD is encouraged in CA as a way to meet targeted methane emission reductions and recover biogas for electricity production or use as transportation fuel (CARB, 2017). Covered lagoon digesters are relatively easy to implement because of their simple design (Montes et al., 2013) and many CA farms already use lagoons and flush-based collection systems (Kaffka et al., 2016). In this study, the biogas was assumed to be combusted onsite in a reciprocating internal combustion engine to provide electricity for the dairy's own use, offsetting electricity from the CA grid.
The AD scenario is based on management practices at CA dairies with
Fig. 2. System boundaries for the AD scenario.

operating covered lagoon digesters from manure collection to end use as a fertilizer (Fig. 2). A process model for the AD scenario was developed using measured parameters from long-term operation of covered lagoon digesters. Parameters such as biogas yield per influent volatile solids (VS), methane content of the biogas, VS reduction, N mineralization, and H2S production were all taken from a year-long study conducted with six digesters in CA (Summers and Williams, 2013). In the AD sce- nario, manure is collected in the same fashion as the baseline. The flushed manure goes through an inclined screen separator to remove a portion of the solids, which are sent to solid storage. The liquid stream continues to the covered lagoon digester where biogas is collected beneath the cover and captured for processing. The biogas is cleaned to remove H2S then sent to the engine generator where electricity is pro- duced for on-farm use. The electrical efficiency of the engine generator was 25%, based on reported values for AD systems (Summers and Wil- liams, 2013). In this study, no heat recovery was assumed. Heat recovery is highly dependent on the location of the biogas combustion equipment and the layout of the dairy (Mayer et al., 2019); some operations may be able to recover heat for hot water use in the milk parlor or other areas of the farm if the locations are close enough, but it may not be feasible on all dairies. Leaks and other process inefficiencies in the biogas pro- cessing system were assumed to cause a 5% loss of produced biogas (CARB, 2022b).

The liquid effluent (digestate) from the digester is used as a nutrient source for the same forage crops as the baseline scenario, and applica- tion is subject to the same N application limit. However, the total area of application will be different due to a different N content in the digestate compared to undigested manure. During storage in an uncovered lagoon, N is emitted as NH3. NH3 is not emitted from the covered lagoon, leading to a higher N content in the digestate. NH3 is emitted during digestate storage, and the amount depends on the duration of storage. In this study, it was assumed that digestate was used quickly, and digestate was not stored outside the digester for more than 30 days. As a result, more N is available during land application, SO a greater area is required to keep N application below the limit.

The NEWIR scenario is intended to capture the impacts associated with the integrated energy, nutrient, and water recovery system. Four NEWIR scenarios are evaluated, each using a different algae species. NEWIR-max uses Spirulina maxima, NEWIR-rein uses Chlamydomonas reinhardtii, NEWIR-vulg uses Chlorella vulgaris, and NEWIR-obliq uses Scenedesmus obliquus. S. maxima was evaluated in a previous LCA and found to contribute to environmental impacts due to chemical produc- tion required to manage pH (Glover et al., 2022). The remaining species were evaluated as alternatives because chemicals are not required for cultivation. The environmental impacts of the remaining species have not yet been evaluated, prompting the need for multiple NEWIR sce- narios in this LCA.

The NEWIR system involves three main unit processes for resource recovery from dairy manure (Glover et al., 2022, Fig. 3). Manure is first treated by HTC, which uses high temperature and pressure to convert manure in a solid carbonaceous material called hydrochar, leaving a portion of the nutrients in the aqueous stream (Reza et al., 2016). Hydrochar is similar in composition to lignite coal and can be burned in a conventional coal power plant to generate electricity in place of fossil coal (Berge et al., 2015; Reza et al., 2014).

The HAP is sent to raceway ponds and used as a nutrient source for microalgae. HAP contains high concentrations of nutrients and soluble organic compounds and must be diluted to support algae growth. In this study, HAP was diluted to 5% by volume to support algae growth based on experimental results. Experiments are further discussed in the Sup- plementary Material. Microalgae are harvested using a filter system and mixed with corn stover to provide a protein-rich feed for the dairy cattle, thereby replacing a portion of soybeans in the cattle diet. A portion of the pond effluent is sent to MD, which uses waste heat from the HTC reactor to clean the water. This water can be used for cleaning and sterilization on the dairy farm, replacing RO systems for on-site pro- duction of high-quality water. The remaining pond effluent is either used for irrigation on the crop fields or sent to disposal.
As explored in a previous study, cultivation of S. maxima requires

Fig. 3. System boundaries for the four NEWIR scenarios: NEWIR-max with S. maxima, NEWIR-rein with C. reinhardtii, NEWIR-vulg with C. vulgaris, and NEWIR-obliq with S. obliquus (*applies to NEWIR-max scenario only).

addition of chemical buffers to raise the pH of HAP to an appropriate range for the species (9.0-9.5) (Richmond and Grobbelaar, 1986). So- dium carbonate and sodium bicarbonate are used to manage pH for S. maxima, which means the pond effluent for this species contains salts and cannot be used for irrigation in the NEWIR-max scenario. The other three species can grow in HAP without adjusting pH. Therefore, the pond effluent in NEWIR-rein, NEWIR-vulg, and NEWIR-obliq does not contain salts and can be used for crop irrigation. Nutrients in the pond effluent not consumed by the algae are available for the crops and meet a portion of crop needs; the remaining nutrients required by the crops are met with synthetic fertilizer. In the NEWIR-max scenario, crop re- quirements are met entirely with synthetic fertilizer. Application of the pond effluent and/or synthetic fertilizer follows N application limits (R5-2013-0122, 2013). The dairy order does not specifically regulate amounts of P applied, SO application of P is assumed to be 1.4 times the P removed in the crop at harvest. In this way, the NEWIR system recovers energy, nutrients, and water from manure while maintaining the same production of forage crops for the dairy.

NEWIR-max uses Spirulina maxima, NEWIR-rein uses Chlamydomonas reinhardtii, NEWIR-vulg uses Chlorella vulgaris, and NEWIR-obliq uses Scenedesmus obliquus. S. maxima was evaluated in a previous LCA and found to contribute to environmental impacts due to chemical produc- tion required to manage pH (Glover et al., 2022). The remaining species were evaluated as alternatives because chemicals are not required for cultivation. The environmental impacts of the remaining species have not yet been evaluated, prompting the need for multiple NEWIR sce- narios in this LCA.

The NEWIR system involves three main unit processes for resource recovery from dairy manure (Glover et al., 2022, Fig. 3). Manure is first treated by HTC, which uses high temperature and pressure to convert manure in a solid carbonaceous material called hydrochar, leaving a portion of the nutrients in the aqueous stream (Reza et al., 2016). Hydrochar is similar in composition to lignite coal and can be burned in a conventional coal power plant to generate electricity in place of fossil coal (Berge et al., 2015; Reza et al., 2014).

The HAP is sent to raceway ponds and used as a nutrient source for microalgae. HAP contains high concentrations of nutrients and soluble organic compounds and must be diluted to support algae growth. In this study, HAP was diluted to 5% by volume to support algae growth based on experimental results. Experiments are further discussed in the Sup- plementary Material. Microalgae are harvested using a filter system and mixed with corn stover to provide a protein-rich feed for the dairy cattle, thereby replacing a portion of soybeans in the cattle diet. A portion of the pond effluent is sent to MD, which uses waste heat from the HTC reactor to clean the water. This water can be used for cleaning and sterilization on the dairy farm, replacing RO systems for on-site pro- duction of high-quality water. The remaining pond effluent is either used for irrigation on the crop fields or sent to disposal.
As explored in a previous study, cultivation of S. maxima requires

Fig. 3. System boundaries for the four NEWIR scenarios: NEWIR-max with S. maxima, NEWIR-rein with C. reinhardtii, NEWIR-vulg with C. vulgaris, and NEWIR-obliq with S. obliquus (*applies to NEWIR-max scenario only).

addition of chemical buffers to raise the pH of HAP to an appropriate range for the species (9.0-9.5) (Richmond and Grobbelaar, 1986). So- dium carbonate and sodium bicarbonate are used to manage pH for S. maxima, which means the pond effluent for this species contains salts and cannot be used for irrigation in the NEWIR-max scenario. The other three species can grow in HAP without adjusting pH. Therefore, the pond effluent in NEWIR-rein, NEWIR-vulg, and NEWIR-obliq does not contain salts and can be used for crop irrigation. Nutrients in the pond effluent not consumed by the algae are available for the crops and meet a portion of crop needs; the remaining nutrients required by the crops are met with synthetic fertilizer. In the NEWIR-max scenario, crop re- quirements are met entirely with synthetic fertilizer. Application of the pond effluent and/or synthetic fertilizer follows N application limits (R5-2013-0122, 2013). The dairy order does not specifically regulate amounts of P applied, SO application of P is assumed to be 1.4 times the P removed in the crop at harvest. In this way, the NEWIR system recovers energy, nutrients, and water from manure while maintaining the same production of forage crops for the dairy.
14: The baseline scenario is intended to capture typical manure man- agement practices for large-scale dairies in CA. The system begins with manure collection on the farm and includes storage and land application of manure (Fig. 1). The farm uses freestall barns to house lactating dairy cows, a common practice on CA dairies (Meyer et al., 2011), with access to open corrals dependent on weather conditions. Manure is collected from the freestall barns and the milk parlor with a flush system and stored as a liquid in an uncovered anaerobic lagoon. Recycled lagoon water is used to flush the freestall barns (Meyer et al., 2011) while fresh water is used to flush the milk parlor areas (Summers and Williams, 2013). Solid manure is collected via scraping from the open corral and transfer lanes and stored in a solid pile. The proportion of manure
Fig. 1. System boundaries for the baseline scenario, which covers typical manure management practices on a large-scale freestall dairy in California.

collected as a liquid versus solid depends on the amount of time the cows spend in each location. At a freestall dairy, the majority of manure is typically collected by flushing (Meyer et al., 2019). In this study, it was assumed that 75% of the manure generated by the herd was collected by flushing and stored as a liquid, and 25% was collected and stored as a solid.

Both liquid and solid manure are used as nutrient sources to grow crops for the herd. In this study, manure nutrients are assumed to be applied to corn and wheat in the summer and winter growing seasons, respectively, to provide a portion of the dairy's forage crops (Miller et al., 2017). Lagoon water is applied to fields throughout the year through the irrigation system, while solids are incorporated into the soil between growing seasons. Application of manure nutrients to crop fields was based on regulations set by the 2013 Dairy Order that allows dairies to apply a maximum of 1.4 times the N harvested in the crop to fields receiving manure (R5-2013-0122, 2013). The applied N may come from multiple sources, including manure, synthetic fertilizer, or irrigation water containing nitrates. Synthetic fertilizer can be a significant contributor to groundwater contamination (Rosenstock et al., 2014), but its application varies significantly among dairies, even for the same crop type (Parsons, 2018). Because of the uncertainty involved in application of synthetic fertilizer, it was assumed in this study that manure was the

sole nutrient source for the crops, so the amount of land cultivated as corn and wheat was based on the available manure N applied at the limit set by the Dairy Order (R5-2013-0122, 2013). In practice, dairy pro- ducers may use nutrients from a combination of sources based on crop needs, timing of application in the crop growth cycle, and nutrient levels present in soil.

The AD scenario is intended to capture typical manure management practices on CA dairy farms that process manure in a covered lagoon digester and use recovered biogas to generate electricity onsite. AD captures methane that would otherwise be released to the atmosphere during anaerobic storage of manure. AD is encouraged in CA as a way to meet targeted methane emission reductions and recover biogas for electricity production or use as transportation fuel (CARB, 2017). Covered lagoon digesters are relatively easy to implement because of their simple design (Montes et al., 2013) and many CA farms already use lagoons and flush-based collection systems (Kaffka et al., 2016). In this study, the biogas was assumed to be combusted onsite in a reciprocating internal combustion engine to provide electricity for the dairy's own use, offsetting electricity from the CA grid.
The AD scenario is based on management practices at CA dairies with
Fig. 2. System boundaries for the AD scenario.

operating covered lagoon digesters from manure collection to end use as a fertilizer (Fig. 2). A process model for the AD scenario was developed using measured parameters from long-term operation of covered lagoon digesters. Parameters such as biogas yield per influent volatile solids (VS), methane content of the biogas, VS reduction, N mineralization, and H2S production were all taken from a year-long study conducted with six digesters in CA (Summers and Williams, 2013). In the AD sce- nario, manure is collected in the same fashion as the baseline. The flushed manure goes through an inclined screen separator to remove a portion of the solids, which are sent to solid storage. The liquid stream continues to the covered lagoon digester where biogas is collected beneath the cover and captured for processing. The biogas is cleaned to remove H2S then sent to the engine generator where electricity is pro- duced for on-farm use. The electrical efficiency of the engine generator was 25%, based on reported values for AD systems (Summers and Wil- liams, 2013). In this study, no heat recovery was assumed. Heat recovery is highly dependent on the location of the biogas combustion equipment and the layout of the dairy (Mayer et al., 2019); some operations may be able to recover heat for hot water use in the milk parlor or other areas of the farm if the locations are close enough, but it may not be feasible on all dairies. Leaks and other process inefficiencies in the biogas pro- cessing system were assumed to cause a 5% loss of produced biogas (CARB, 2022b).

The liquid effluent (digestate) from the digester is used as a nutrient source for the same forage crops as the baseline scenario, and applica- tion is subject to the same N application limit. However, the total area of application will be different due to a different N content in the digestate compared to undigested manure. During storage in an uncovered lagoon, N is emitted as NH3. NH3 is not emitted from the covered lagoon, leading to a higher N content in the digestate. NH3 is emitted during digestate storage, and the amount depends on the duration of storage. In this study, it was assumed that digestate was used quickly, and digestate was not stored outside the digester for more than 30 days. As a result, more N is available during land application, SO a greater area is required to keep N application below the limit.

Fig. 1. System boundaries for the baseline scenario, which covers typical manure management practices on a large-scale freestall dairy in California.

collected as a liquid versus solid depends on the amount of time the cows spend in each location. At a freestall dairy, the majority of manure is typically collected by flushing (Meyer et al., 2019). In this study, it was assumed that 75% of the manure generated by the herd was collected by flushing and stored as a liquid, and 25% was collected and stored as a solid.

Both liquid and solid manure are used as nutrient sources to grow crops for the herd. In this study, manure nutrients are assumed to be applied to corn and wheat in the summer and winter growing seasons, respectively, to provide a portion of the dairy's forage crops (Miller et al., 2017). Lagoon water is applied to fields throughout the year through the irrigation system, while solids are incorporated into the soil between growing seasons. Application of manure nutrients to crop fields was based on regulations set by the 2013 Dairy Order that allows dairies to apply a maximum of 1.4 times the N harvested in the crop to fields receiving manure (R5-2013-0122, 2013). The applied N may come from multiple sources, including manure, synthetic fertilizer, or irrigation water containing nitrates. Synthetic fertilizer can be a significant contributor to groundwater contamination (Rosenstock et al., 2014), but its application varies significantly among dairies, even for the same crop type (Parsons, 2018). Because of the uncertainty involved in application of synthetic fertilizer, it was assumed in this study that manure was the

sole nutrient source for the crops, so the amount of land cultivated as corn and wheat was based on the available manure N applied at the limit set by the Dairy Order (R5-2013-0122, 2013). In practice, dairy pro- ducers may use nutrients from a combination of sources based on crop needs, timing of application in the crop growth cycle, and nutrient levels present in soil.

The AD scenario is intended to capture typical manure management practices on CA dairy farms that process manure in a covered lagoon digester and use recovered biogas to generate electricity onsite. AD captures methane that would otherwise be released to the atmosphere during anaerobic storage of manure. AD is encouraged in CA as a way to meet targeted methane emission reductions and recover biogas for electricity production or use as transportation fuel (CARB, 2017). Covered lagoon digesters are relatively easy to implement because of their simple design (Montes et al., 2013) and many CA farms already use lagoons and flush-based collection systems (Kaffka et al., 2016). In this study, the biogas was assumed to be combusted onsite in a reciprocating internal combustion engine to provide electricity for the dairy's own use, offsetting electricity from the CA grid.
The AD scenario is based on management practices at CA dairies with
Fig. 2. System boundaries for the AD scenario.

operating covered lagoon digesters from manure collection to end use as a fertilizer (Fig. 2). A process model for the AD scenario was developed using measured parameters from long-term operation of covered lagoon digesters. Parameters such as biogas yield per influent volatile solids (VS), methane content of the biogas, VS reduction, N mineralization, and H2S production were all taken from a year-long study conducted with six digesters in CA (Summers and Williams, 2013). In the AD sce- nario, manure is collected in the same fashion as the baseline. The flushed manure goes through an inclined screen separator to remove a portion of the solids, which are sent to solid storage. The liquid stream continues to the covered lagoon digester where biogas is collected beneath the cover and captured for processing. The biogas is cleaned to remove H2S then sent to the engine generator where electricity is pro- duced for on-farm use. The electrical efficiency of the engine generator was 25%, based on reported values for AD systems (Summers and Wil- liams, 2013). In this study, no heat recovery was assumed. Heat recovery is highly dependent on the location of the biogas combustion equipment and the layout of the dairy (Mayer et al., 2019); some operations may be able to recover heat for hot water use in the milk parlor or other areas of the farm if the locations are close enough, but it may not be feasible on all dairies. Leaks and other process inefficiencies in the biogas pro- cessing system were assumed to cause a 5% loss of produced biogas (CARB, 2022b).

The liquid effluent (digestate) from the digester is used as a nutrient source for the same forage crops as the baseline scenario, and applica- tion is subject to the same N application limit. However, the total area of application will be different due to a different N content in the digestate compared to undigested manure. During storage in an uncovered lagoon, N is emitted as NH3. NH3 is not emitted from the covered lagoon, leading to a higher N content in the digestate. NH3 is emitted during digestate storage, and the amount depends on the duration of storage. In this study, it was assumed that digestate was used quickly, and digestate was not stored outside the digester for more than 30 days. As a result, more N is available during land application, SO a greater area is required to keep N application below the limit.

The NEWIR scenario is intended to capture the impacts associated with the integrated energy, nutrient, and water recovery system. Four NEWIR scenarios are evaluated, each using a different algae species. NEWIR-max uses Spirulina maxima, NEWIR-rein uses Chlamydomonas reinhardtii, NEWIR-vulg uses Chlorella vulgaris, and NEWIR-obliq uses Scenedesmus obliquus. S. maxima was evaluated in a previous LCA and found to contribute to environmental impacts due to chemical produc- tion required to manage pH (Glover et al., 2022). The remaining species were evaluated as alternatives because chemicals are not required for cultivation. The environmental impacts of the remaining species have not yet been evaluated, prompting the need for multiple NEWIR sce- narios in this LCA.

The NEWIR system involves three main unit processes for resource recovery from dairy manure (Glover et al., 2022, Fig. 3). Manure is first treated by HTC, which uses high temperature and pressure to convert manure in a solid carbonaceous material called hydrochar, leaving a portion of the nutrients in the aqueous stream (Reza et al., 2016). Hydrochar is similar in composition to lignite coal and can be burned in a conventional coal power plant to generate electricity in place of fossil coal (Berge et al., 2015; Reza et al., 2014).

The HAP is sent to raceway ponds and used as a nutrient source for microalgae. HAP contains high concentrations of nutrients and soluble organic compounds and must be diluted to support algae growth. In this study, HAP was diluted to 5% by volume to support algae growth based on experimental results. Experiments are further discussed in the Sup- plementary Material. Microalgae are harvested using a filter system and mixed with corn stover to provide a protein-rich feed for the dairy cattle, thereby replacing a portion of soybeans in the cattle diet. A portion of the pond effluent is sent to MD, which uses waste heat from the HTC reactor to clean the water. This water can be used for cleaning and sterilization on the dairy farm, replacing RO systems for on-site pro- duction of high-quality water. The remaining pond effluent is either used for irrigation on the crop fields or sent to disposal.
As explored in a previous study, cultivation of S. maxima requires

Fig. 3. System boundaries for the four NEWIR scenarios: NEWIR-max with S. maxima, NEWIR-rein with C. reinhardtii, NEWIR-vulg with C. vulgaris, and NEWIR-obliq with S. obliquus (*applies to NEWIR-max scenario only).

addition of chemical buffers to raise the pH of HAP to an appropriate range for the species (9.0-9.5) (Richmond and Grobbelaar, 1986). So- dium carbonate and sodium bicarbonate are used to manage pH for S. maxima, which means the pond effluent for this species contains salts and cannot be used for irrigation in the NEWIR-max scenario. The other three species can grow in HAP without adjusting pH. Therefore, the pond effluent in NEWIR-rein, NEWIR-vulg, and NEWIR-obliq does not contain salts and can be used for crop irrigation. Nutrients in the pond effluent not consumed by the algae are available for the crops and meet a portion of crop needs; the remaining nutrients required by the crops are met with synthetic fertilizer. In the NEWIR-max scenario, crop re- quirements are met entirely with synthetic fertilizer. Application of the pond effluent and/or synthetic fertilizer follows N application limits (R5-2013-0122, 2013). The dairy order does not specifically regulate amounts of P applied, SO application of P is assumed to be 1.4 times the P removed in the crop at harvest. In this way, the NEWIR system recovers energy, nutrients, and water from manure while maintaining the same production of forage crops for the dairy.

The liquid effluent (digestate) from the digester is used as a nutrient source for the same forage crops as the baseline scenario, and applica- tion is subject to the same N application limit. However, the total area of application will be different due to a different N content in the digestate compared to undigested manure. During storage in an uncovered lagoon, N is emitted as NH3. NH3 is not emitted from the covered lagoon, leading to a higher N content in the digestate. NH3 is emitted during digestate storage, and the amount depends on the duration of storage. In this study, it was assumed that digestate was used quickly, and digestate was not stored outside the digester for more than 30 days. As a result, more N is available during land application, SO a greater area is required to keep N application below the limit.

The NEWIR scenario is intended to capture the impacts associated with the integrated energy, nutrient, and water recovery system. Four NEWIR scenarios are evaluated, each using a different algae species. NEWIR-max uses Spirulina maxima, NEWIR-rein uses Chlamydomonas reinhardtii, NEWIR-vulg uses Chlorella vulgaris, and NEWIR-obliq uses Scenedesmus obliquus. S. maxima was evaluated in a previous LCA and found to contribute to environmental impacts due to chemical produc- tion required to manage pH (Glover et al., 2022). The remaining species were evaluated as alternatives because chemicals are not required for cultivation. The environmental impacts of the remaining species have not yet been evaluated, prompting the need for multiple NEWIR sce- narios in this LCA.

The NEWIR system involves three main unit processes for resource recovery from dairy manure (Glover et al., 2022, Fig. 3). Manure is first treated by HTC, which uses high temperature and pressure to convert manure in a solid carbonaceous material called hydrochar, leaving a portion of the nutrients in the aqueous stream (Reza et al., 2016). Hydrochar is similar in composition to lignite coal and can be burned in a conventional coal power plant to generate electricity in place of fossil coal (Berge et al., 2015; Reza et al., 2014).

The HAP is sent to raceway ponds and used as a nutrient source for microalgae. HAP contains high concentrations of nutrients and soluble organic compounds and must be diluted to support algae growth. In this study, HAP was diluted to 5% by volume to support algae growth based on experimental results. Experiments are further discussed in the Sup- plementary Material. Microalgae are harvested using a filter system and mixed with corn stover to provide a protein-rich feed for the dairy cattle, thereby replacing a portion of soybeans in the cattle diet. A portion of the pond effluent is sent to MD, which uses waste heat from the HTC reactor to clean the water. This water can be used for cleaning and sterilization on the dairy farm, replacing RO systems for on-site pro- duction of high-quality water. The remaining pond effluent is either used for irrigation on the crop fields or sent to disposal.
As explored in a previous study, cultivation of S. maxima requires

Fig. 3. System boundaries for the four NEWIR scenarios: NEWIR-max with S. maxima, NEWIR-rein with C. reinhardtii, NEWIR-vulg with C. vulgaris, and NEWIR-obliq with S. obliquus (*applies to NEWIR-max scenario only).

addition of chemical buffers to raise the pH of HAP to an appropriate range for the species (9.0-9.5) (Richmond and Grobbelaar, 1986). So- dium carbonate and sodium bicarbonate are used to manage pH for S. maxima, which means the pond effluent for this species contains salts and cannot be used for irrigation in the NEWIR-max scenario. The other three species can grow in HAP without adjusting pH. Therefore, the pond effluent in NEWIR-rein, NEWIR-vulg, and NEWIR-obliq does not contain salts and can be used for crop irrigation. Nutrients in the pond effluent not consumed by the algae are available for the crops and meet a portion of crop needs; the remaining nutrients required by the crops are met with synthetic fertilizer. In the NEWIR-max scenario, crop re- quirements are met entirely with synthetic fertilizer. Application of the pond effluent and/or synthetic fertilizer follows N application limits (R5-2013-0122, 2013). The dairy order does not specifically regulate amounts of P applied, SO application of P is assumed to be 1.4 times the P removed in the crop at harvest. In this way, the NEWIR system recovers energy, nutrients, and water from manure while maintaining the same production of forage crops for the dairy.
21: The baseline scenario is intended to capture typical manure man- agement practices for large-scale dairies in CA. The system begins with manure collection on the farm and includes storage and land application of manure (Fig. 1). The farm uses freestall barns to house lactating dairy cows, a common practice on CA dairies (Meyer et al., 2011), with access to open corrals dependent on weather conditions. Manure is collected from the freestall barns and the milk parlor with a flush system and stored as a liquid in an uncovered anaerobic lagoon. Recycled lagoon water is used to flush the freestall barns (Meyer et al., 2011) while fresh water is used to flush the milk parlor areas (Summers and Williams, 2013). Solid manure is collected via scraping from the open corral and transfer lanes and stored in a solid pile. The proportion of manure
Fig. 1. System boundaries for the baseline scenario, which covers typical manure management practices on a large-scale freestall dairy in California.

collected as a liquid versus solid depends on the amount of time the cows spend in each location. At a freestall dairy, the majority of manure is typically collected by flushing (Meyer et al., 2019). In this study, it was assumed that 75% of the manure generated by the herd was collected by flushing and stored as a liquid, and 25% was collected and stored as a solid.

Both liquid and solid manure are used as nutrient sources to grow crops for the herd. In this study, manure nutrients are assumed to be applied to corn and wheat in the summer and winter growing seasons, respectively, to provide a portion of the dairy's forage crops (Miller et al., 2017). Lagoon water is applied to fields throughout the year through the irrigation system, while solids are incorporated into the soil between growing seasons. Application of manure nutrients to crop fields was based on regulations set by the 2013 Dairy Order that allows dairies to apply a maximum of 1.4 times the N harvested in the crop to fields receiving manure (R5-2013-0122, 2013). The applied N may come from multiple sources, including manure, synthetic fertilizer, or irrigation water containing nitrates. Synthetic fertilizer can be a significant contributor to groundwater contamination (Rosenstock et al., 2014), but its application varies significantly among dairies, even for the same crop type (Parsons, 2018). Because of the uncertainty involved in application of synthetic fertilizer, it was assumed in this study that manure was the

sole nutrient source for the crops, so the amount of land cultivated as corn and wheat was based on the available manure N applied at the limit set by the Dairy Order (R5-2013-0122, 2013). In practice, dairy pro- ducers may use nutrients from a combination of sources based on crop needs, timing of application in the crop growth cycle, and nutrient levels present in soil.

The AD scenario is intended to capture typical manure management practices on CA dairy farms that process manure in a covered lagoon digester and use recovered biogas to generate electricity onsite. AD captures methane that would otherwise be released to the atmosphere during anaerobic storage of manure. AD is encouraged in CA as a way to meet targeted methane emission reductions and recover biogas for electricity production or use as transportation fuel (CARB, 2017). Covered lagoon digesters are relatively easy to implement because of their simple design (Montes et al., 2013) and many CA farms already use lagoons and flush-based collection systems (Kaffka et al., 2016). In this study, the biogas was assumed to be combusted onsite in a reciprocating internal combustion engine to provide electricity for the dairy's own use, offsetting electricity from the CA grid.
The AD scenario is based on management practices at CA dairies with
Fig. 2. System boundaries for the AD scenario.

operating covered lagoon digesters from manure collection to end use as a fertilizer (Fig. 2). A process model for the AD scenario was developed using measured parameters from long-term operation of covered lagoon digesters. Parameters such as biogas yield per influent volatile solids (VS), methane content of the biogas, VS reduction, N mineralization, and H2S production were all taken from a year-long study conducted with six digesters in CA (Summers and Williams, 2013). In the AD sce- nario, manure is collected in the same fashion as the baseline. The flushed manure goes through an inclined screen separator to remove a portion of the solids, which are sent to solid storage. The liquid stream continues to the covered lagoon digester where biogas is collected beneath the cover and captured for processing. The biogas is cleaned to remove H2S then sent to the engine generator where electricity is pro- duced for on-farm use. The electrical efficiency of the engine generator was 25%, based on reported values for AD systems (Summers and Wil- liams, 2013). In this study, no heat recovery was assumed. Heat recovery is highly dependent on the location of the biogas combustion equipment and the layout of the dairy (Mayer et al., 2019); some operations may be able to recover heat for hot water use in the milk parlor or other areas of the farm if the locations are close enough, but it may not be feasible on all dairies. Leaks and other process inefficiencies in the biogas pro- cessing system were assumed to cause a 5% loss of produced biogas (CARB, 2022b).

The liquid effluent (digestate) from the digester is used as a nutrient source for the same forage crops as the baseline scenario, and applica- tion is subject to the same N application limit. However, the total area of application will be different due to a different N content in the digestate compared to undigested manure. During storage in an uncovered lagoon, N is emitted as NH3. NH3 is not emitted from the covered lagoon, leading to a higher N content in the digestate. NH3 is emitted during digestate storage, and the amount depends on the duration of storage. In this study, it was assumed that digestate was used quickly, and digestate was not stored outside the digester for more than 30 days. As a result, more N is available during land application, SO a greater area is required to keep N application below the limit.

Application of the pond effluent and/or synthetic fertilizer follows N application limits (R5-2013-0122, 2013). The dairy order does not specifically regulate amounts of P applied, SO application of P is assumed to be 1.4 times the P removed in the crop at harvest. In this way, the NEWIR system recovers energy, nutrients, and water from manure while maintaining the same production of forage crops for the dairy.
24: The LCI includes all inputs and outputs for the treatment system covered in each scenario. Inputs include dairy manure, water, electricity and fuel use, chemicals, and materials (Table 1). Outputs include envi- ronmental emissions to air, water, and soil, along with recovered products. Production of energy sources and animal feeds that are replaced by recovered products are included as avoided products to assess environmental benefits from resource recovery. Ecoinvent version 2.6 and US LCI databases were used to model upstream processes such as electricity generation, fuel production and combustion, and chemical and materials manufacturing (US LCI, 2012; Wernet et al., 2016). Manure production and composition was determined using established
equations and milk production data reported for large-scale commercial dairies in the Central Valley (Table S1). The same quantity and composition of manure was used in all scenarios.
25: In the baseline inventory, conventional manure management prac- tices are based on farm surveys, technical reports, and published liter- ature for large-scale CA dairy farms (Capareda et al., 2010; Chang et al., 2005; Kaffka et al., 2016; Meyer et al., 2011). The impacts for upstream production of diesel fuel and electricity come from the Ecoinvent version 3.6 database, and electricity production was modeled using the CA en- ergy mix in Ecoinvent. Emissions released from manure during each stage of manure management were calculated using established emis- sion factors and process modeling (U.S. EPA, 2017; IPCC, 2006; Rotz and Oenema, 2006). Methods are covered in the complete baseline inventory in Table S2.

In the AD inventory, typical practices for covered lagoon digesters were taken from technical reports detailing operation of existing digester systems at CA dairies. Digester parameters such as biogas pro- duction per influent VS, N mineralization, and methane content of the biogas were taken from operation of a covered lagoon digester treating only manure and flush water (Summers and Williams, 2013). Electricity for biogas cleaning and emissions associated with biogas combustion were taken from the literature (Aguirre-Villegas et al., 2014; Boulamanti et al., 2013). Emissions from collection and solid manure storage were calculated using the same modeling methods as the baseline (U.S. EPA, 2017; IPCC, 2006; Rotz and Oenema, 2006). Additional details are provided with the complete inventory in Table S3.
Summarized inventory data for LCA scenarios. Complete life cycle inventories are given in the Supplementary Material.

30: In the NEWIR scenarios, inventory data is developed from a combi- nation of experimental results, literature sources, and process modeling (Table S4). A full-scale process model was developed in the ChemCAD simulation software (version 7.1.4.10142). Experimental data for the HTC process, including distribution of solid, liquid, and gaseous prod- ucts, was taken from Reza et al. (2016). Infrastructure for an HTC reactor was based on an existing HTC system (Owsianiak et al., 2016). Algae productivity and biomass composition for all four species was taken from experimental results (Table S5). Chemicals for algae culti- vation (NEWIR-max scenario only) were based on experimental results. Operation of raceway ponds was based on full-scale systems (Clarens et al., 2010; Dassey et al., 2014). Production of water through MD was based on experiments presented in Silva and Hiibel (2021) and heat and mass transfer modeling. Additional details of the NEWIR data sources are provided with the complete inventory in Table S4.

Upstream impacts associated with material and chemical manufacturing, as well as water, diesel fuel, natural gas, and electricity production and use, came from Ecoinvent version 3.6 and the US LCI databases (Wernet et al., 2016; US LCI, 2012). Materials include steel infrastructure for the HTC reactor, separation equipment, and hydrochar processing, as well as high-density polyethylene pond liner for the algae pond. Chemical inputs include sodium carbonate and sodium bicar- bonate which are used to manage pH in the algae pond in the NEWIR-max scenario only. Electricity from the CA production mix is used to operate pumps and filtration equipment. Natural gas is used to heat the HTC reactor. Water inputs were based on dilution of HAP to 5% by volume for algae cultivation as well as a small water input for HTC. The HTC experiments used in this study were based on a 1:10 ratio of solids to liquids in the incoming manure slurry, so additional water was included in the LCA to ensure the ratio was reached. In practice, a flush-based manure collection system likely provides adequate water for the HTC system. Emissions include those released during collection and short-term storage of manure prior to the HTC reaction. CO2 released from the HTC reaction and from hydrochar combustion is considered biogenic (Berge et al., 2015; Reza et al., 2016) and not included in the global warming potential (GWP) calculation.

In addition to the inputs, the AD and four NEWIR scenarios account for benefits of energy and nutrient recovery through avoided products. The energy recovered in these scenarios replaces a conventional energy source, either CA grid electricity in AD (replaced by electricity from biogas combustion) or fossil coal in NEWIR (replaced by electricity from hydrochar combustion). NEWIR provides an additional benefit through the algae cattle feed, which replaces soybeans in the cattle diet. The amount of grid electricity and fossil coal avoided is based on the equivalent amount of energy produced from the alternative source. The amount of soybean replaced is based on the equivalent amount of pro- tein available in the algae. The avoided products are included in SimaPro with the rest of the LCI and reported with the results in each scenario.

33: The LCA was performed using SimaPro PhD version 9.1.1.1 for impact assessment using the ReCiPe Midpoint (H) method (Huijbregts et al., 2017). Four categories were selected for discussion in this study: GWP, marine eutrophication potential (MEP), freshwater eutrophica- tion potential (FEP), and water consumption (WC). Each category is related to dairy manure management and energy, nutrient, and water recovery from waste. GWP includes GHG emissions in kg CO2-equivalent (eq.) associated with conventional dairy manure management and recovered energy in AD and NEWIR. MEP and FEP capture nutrient pollution leading to eutrophication and are expressed as kg N-eq. and kg P-eq., respectively. WC represents water usage expressed in m³ of water consumed. All results are reported per functional unit of 1000 kg raw manure. Impacts are reported using a gross total, which does not include

avoided products, and a net total, which includes all impacts and avoi- ded products, in each category. These impact categories were selected to capture key challenges related to the water-energy-nutrient nexus in manure management.
34: Inventory values were varied over defined distributions for 1000 iterations using the Monte Carlo uncertainty simulation in SimaPro to determine an uncertainty range in each impact category due to changes in the inventory inputs. The distributions were assumed to be uniform between a maximum and minimum value for each inventory value. When available, maximum and minimum values were taken from the source of the inventory data, including survey data, experimental re- sults, reported results (lagoon digester operation and parameters), and uncertainty ranges provided with emission modeling methods. When specific information regarding the max/min range was not available, a default value of + 10% was assumed (Aguirre-Villegas et al., 2014). Uncertainty is reported using the standard deviation of the uncertainty distribution and plotted as error bars in the results.

A sensitivity analysis was also performed. Each inventory input was varied by + 10% individually and the impact categories were recalcu- lated. Percent change in the impact category was calculated for each inventory input to determine which inputs the impact category was most sensitive to. Methods, results, and discussion are included in the Sup- plementary Material (Figs. S1-S6).
35: Of the six scenarios evaluated, the baseline scenario representing conventional manure management practices has the highest net total GWP of 361 + 18.0 kg CO2-eq. per 1000 kg manure functional unit (Fig. 4). Manure storage is the largest contributor to the total baseline GWP at 90.8%, with emissions from the anaerobic lagoon, which are dominated by methane, the majority contributing 87% of the total baseline GWP. Thus, reducing manure storage in anaerobic conditions has the greatest potential for reducing GWP. Solid storage contributes only 3.3% of the total GWP. Land application, which includes nitrous oxide emissions from manure application as well as production and combustion of diesel fuel to operate machinery, and collection each have a smaller contribution of 7.4% and 1.8%, respectively. Collection in- cludes GHG emissions from manure, fuel use, and operation of the RO system to provide clean water for the milk processing equipment.

Compared to the baseline, AD substantially reduces GWP from manure management with a gross total of 73.4 + 5.7 kg CO2-eq. When the benefits of energy recovery from biogas are considered, the net total is reduced to 63.0 + 7.0 kg CO2-eq. The dominant contributor is land application (40.9%), followed by the digestion process (25.3%) and storage (25.0%). Storage emissions come from scraped solid manure and separated solids, which do not enter the digester. Lagoon emissions are avoided because the lagoon digester is covered, reducing the overall GWP significantly relative to the baseline. The AD process emissions include leaks and inefficiencies in the digester and biogas processing equipment, as well as emissions from digestate storage that account for about half of the AD unit process GWP or 12.9% of the gross total for the scenario. This GWP primarily comes from methane, which is emitted due to residual VS not consumed during digestion, as well as nitrous oxide. Reducing the length of digestate storage or covering the digestate storage pond, as done in some biogas projects (Summers and Williams, 2013), can help reduce the emissions associated with this step. In land application, emissions are higher than the baseline due to increased nitrous oxide emissions (22.6 CO2-eq. in AD versus 19.4 kg CO2-eq. in baseline). During AD, ammonia emissions are reduced compared to the uncovered lagoon in the baseline, leading to a higher N content in the
[ ] Collection|[ ] HTC|[ ] Algae Cultivation [ ] Storage|[ ] Grid Electricity Offset|[ ] Coal Offset [ ] Soybean Offset [ ] AD|[ ] Land Application|[ ] Net Total
Fig. 4. Results for global warming potential (GWP) of each scenario. Error bars represent the standard deviation obtained from uncertainty analysis.
digestate as compared to the lagoon effluent resulting in greater N emissions as nitrous oxide and ammonia during both digestate storage and land application.

The AD scenario also provides a benefit to GWP in the form of an avoided product - CA grid electricity that has been replaced by elec- tricity from biogas. 10.4 kg CO2-eq. is avoided through use of biogas for electricity production instead of the grid (Fig. 4).

The results for both the AD and baseline scenarios demonstrate the effectiveness of AD as a GHG mitigation method, consistent with the literature (Naranjo et al., 2020; Owen and Silver, 2015; Aguirre-Villegas and Larson, 2017; Thoma et al., 2012). Many studies report a high contribution to GWP from manure storage, specifically methane from lagoons. Naranjo et al. (2020) conducted an LCA comparing the envi- ronmental impacts of milk production in CA between 1964 and 2014 to determine the effects of different farm management practices. In the 2014 models, manure management accounted for 40.5-41.1% of the total GWP associated with milk production in CA, primarily due to methane emissions from anaerobic lagoons. Field measurements of emissions at large-scale dairies in the western US have also shown that lagoons and slurry systems have the highest GWP of any manure storage method, and the mean contribution from lagoons was 20 times higher than the mean contribution from manure storage (Owen and Silver, 2015). Lagoons have a much higher methane conversion factor than solid storage, making liquid storage in lagoons one of the key contrib- utors to GWP. A GHG assessment of milk production in the US found that

regions with a high prevalence of anaerobic lagoons had a higher contribution from manure management (Thoma et al., 2012). In addi- tion, a survey of Wisconsin dairies performed found that larger dairies tended to handle liquid manure, which had a greater contribution to GHG emissions than solid storage (Aguirre-Villegas and Larson, 2017).

Replacing liquid manure storage with AD has been shown to reduce GWP. In a study covering different manure processing pathways for Wisconsin dairy farms, it was determined that AD reduced GWP by 48% relative to a base case that included lagoon storage (Aguirre-Villegas et al., 2014). The AD scenario included benefits from avoided fossil CO2 when grid electricity was replaced by biogas electricity. In CA, a study conducted for CARB showed that all types of AD systems, including lagoon digesters, resulted in GHG reductions compared to conventional storage in a lagoon (Kaffka et al., 2016). AD is one of the most promising methods to reach emission reduction targets set by Senate Bill 1383 in CA, and current AD projects funded through the Dairy Digester Research and Development Program are expected to provide 2.0 MMT CO2-eq. in emission reductions per year (CARB, 2022a). However, more progress is needed to meet emission targets. With current AD systems and projected cow population reduction, only half of the targeted emission reduction will be met in 2030 (CARB, 2022a). CARB projections show additional AD systems and alternative manure management strategies are needed to meet the targeted reductions. Alternatively, technologies with greater GWP reduction potential could help meet the targeted emissions for dairy farms.

In the NEWIR scenarios, the type of algae has a major effect on the total GWP. Cultivation of S. maxima has a high contribution to GWP, bringing the NEWIR-max scenario to the highest gross total of 479 + 13.4 kg CO2-eq., higher than the baseline scenario total. Production of sodium carbonate and sodium bicarbonate to maintain a pH of 9.5 in the S. maxima pond is the dominant contributor at 90% of the NEWIR-max gross total. Manufacturing these chemicals has a high GWP, primarily due to fossil energy sources used in the production process (US LCI, 2012; Wernet et al., 2016). In the NEWIR-max scenario, the other unit processes have a relatively small contribution. HTC accounts for only 2.0% (9.5 kg CO2-eq.) of the gross total; most of the HTC impact comes from combustion of natural gas to heat the reactor. Short-term storage of manure prior to HTC contributes 2.5%. Synthetic fertilizer, which is used to grow corn and wheat because the pond effluent cannot be land applied due to presence of the chemical buffers, contributes 1.6% of the total GWP.

In the other three NEWIR scenarios, GWP is substantially lower than NEWIR-max as well as the baseline and AD scenarios. NEWIR-rein, NEWIR-vulg, and NEWIR-obliq have similar gross total GWPs of 44.2 + 3.0, 42.2 + 2.9, and 42.2 + 3.6 kg CO2-eq., respectively. The algae species grown in these scenarios do not require pH adjustment of HAP, eliminating the need for chemical additions. Algae cultivation still contributes to GWP through electricity use for paddlewheel mixing in the raceway ponds, biomass harvesting, and pumping, but the contri- bution is much smaller with 25-26% of the gross total GWP in each scenario. HTC and short-term storage have the same magnitude GWP as in NEWIR-max, but the relative contribution is higher due to the lower gross total; HTC contributes 21.4-22.4% and storage contributes 27.4-28.7% of the total depending on the scenario. With respect to land application, the pond effluents from cultivation of C. reinhardtii, C. vul- garis, and S. obliquus can all be used for irrigation because there are no buffer salts added. Nutrients that are not removed by the algae are therefore available to plants as fertilizer, reducing demand for synthetic fertilizer relative to NEWIR-max. The amount of synthetic fertilizer varies depending on the amount of N and P remaining in the pond effluent. C. reinhardtii had the highest nutrient removal and therefore requires the most synthetic fertilizer to reach crop demands, while S. obliquus had the lowest removal. Fertilizer contributed 11.3% of the NEWIR-rein total, 9.4% of the NEWIR-vulg total, and 9.8% of the NEWIR-obliq total.
In each NEWIR scenario, there are also benefits to GWP through

resource recovery. Electricity produced from coal is replaced by elec- tricity from hydrochar and soybean in the cow diet is replaced with algae-based cattle feed. The amount of hydrochar produced is the same in all four NEWIR scenarios, providing a benefit of 71.4 kg CO2-eq. (Fig. 4). Much greater energy recovery is possible through HTC and hydrochar than through AD and biogas, as seen by the difference in GWP offsets. These results are supported by experimental studies where HTC of dairy manure recovers more than twice the amount of energy as AD (Marin-Batista et al., 2020). Other studies have identified GWP benefits when hydrochar replaces coal for electricity production (Benavente et al., 2017; Berge et al., 2015). In a study evaluating HTC for food waste processing, using hydrochar for electricity production improved GWP relative to coal and the average US energy mix (Berge et al., 2015).

The amount of soybean that can be replaced with algae is dependent on the algae species since each species has unique biomass productivity and protein content when grown in HAP. Although algae cultivation is intended for nutrient recovery, it provides benefits to GWP by avoiding conventional soybean production. S. maxima has the highest biomass productivity and second highest protein content, resulting in the greatest soybean offset of 134 kg CO2-eq. avoided per functional unit. C. reinhardtii has the second highest productivity and highest protein content, providing a soybean offset of 110 kg CO2-eq. C. vulgaris and S. obliquus had lower productivities and protein contents, resulting in soybean offsets of 56.2 and 37.8 kg CO2-eq., respectively.

When avoided products are considered, the lowest net total GWP is achieved in the NEWIR-rein scenario. This scenario includes benefits from both recovered energy and nutrients with the lowest total inputs (i. e., no chemicals required for algae cultivation) resulting in net negative GWP of -137 kg CO2-eq. per functional unit. NEWIR-vulg and NEWIR- obliq also result in net negative GWP with -84.9 and -67.0 kg CO2-eq., respectively. While the inputs for these three scenarios are very similar, C. reinhartii achieves greater biomass productivity, resulting in greater offsets through avoided soybean. The net total for NEWIR-max is significantly higher at 275 kg CO2-eq.

The net totals for AD and all NEWIR scenarios are lower than the baseline scenario, indicating that resource recovery improves GHG emissions relative to conventional manure management practices. The best system between AD and NEWIR depends on the algae species selected in NEWIR. AD has a much lower net total than NEWIR-max (63.0 VS 275 kg CO2-eq.), but NEWIR-rein, NEWIR-vulg, and NEWIR- obliq all perform better than AD by a substantial margin. These results indicate that integrated energy and nutrient recovery through HTC and algae cultivation can provide greater benefits to GWP than AD with the right process selection.
