These results indicate that integrated energy and nutrient recovery through HTC and algae cultivation can provide greater benefits to GWP than AD with the right process selection.
38: Of the six scenarios evaluated, the baseline scenario representing conventional manure management practices has the highest net total GWP of 361 + 18.0 kg CO2-eq. per 1000 kg manure functional unit (Fig. 4). Manure storage is the largest contributor to the total baseline GWP at 90.8%, with emissions from the anaerobic lagoon, which are dominated by methane, the majority contributing 87% of the total baseline GWP. Thus, reducing manure storage in anaerobic conditions has the greatest potential for reducing GWP. Solid storage contributes only 3.3% of the total GWP. Land application, which includes nitrous oxide emissions from manure application as well as production and combustion of diesel fuel to operate machinery, and collection each have a smaller contribution of 7.4% and 1.8%, respectively. Collection in- cludes GHG emissions from manure, fuel use, and operation of the RO system to provide clean water for the milk processing equipment.

Compared to the baseline, AD substantially reduces GWP from manure management with a gross total of 73.4 + 5.7 kg CO2-eq. When the benefits of energy recovery from biogas are considered, the net total is reduced to 63.0 + 7.0 kg CO2-eq. The dominant contributor is land application (40.9%), followed by the digestion process (25.3%) and storage (25.0%). Storage emissions come from scraped solid manure and separated solids, which do not enter the digester. Lagoon emissions are avoided because the lagoon digester is covered, reducing the overall GWP significantly relative to the baseline. The AD process emissions include leaks and inefficiencies in the digester and biogas processing equipment, as well as emissions from digestate storage that account for about half of the AD unit process GWP or 12.9% of the gross total for the scenario. This GWP primarily comes from methane, which is emitted due to residual VS not consumed during digestion, as well as nitrous oxide. Reducing the length of digestate storage or covering the digestate storage pond, as done in some biogas projects (Summers and Williams, 2013), can help reduce the emissions associated with this step. In land application, emissions are higher than the baseline due to increased nitrous oxide emissions (22.6 CO2-eq. in AD versus 19.4 kg CO2-eq. in baseline). During AD, ammonia emissions are reduced compared to the uncovered lagoon in the baseline, leading to a higher N content in the
[ ] Collection|[ ] HTC|[ ] Algae Cultivation [ ] Storage|[ ] Grid Electricity Offset|[ ] Coal Offset [ ] Soybean Offset [ ] AD|[ ] Land Application|[ ] Net Total
Fig. 4. Results for global warming potential (GWP) of each scenario. Error bars represent the standard deviation obtained from uncertainty analysis.
digestate as compared to the lagoon effluent resulting in greater N emissions as nitrous oxide and ammonia during both digestate storage and land application.

The AD scenario also provides a benefit to GWP in the form of an avoided product - CA grid electricity that has been replaced by elec- tricity from biogas. 10.4 kg CO2-eq. is avoided through use of biogas for electricity production instead of the grid (Fig. 4).

The results for both the AD and baseline scenarios demonstrate the effectiveness of AD as a GHG mitigation method, consistent with the literature (Naranjo et al., 2020; Owen and Silver, 2015; Aguirre-Villegas and Larson, 2017; Thoma et al., 2012). Many studies report a high contribution to GWP from manure storage, specifically methane from lagoons. Naranjo et al. (2020) conducted an LCA comparing the envi- ronmental impacts of milk production in CA between 1964 and 2014 to determine the effects of different farm management practices. In the 2014 models, manure management accounted for 40.5-41.1% of the total GWP associated with milk production in CA, primarily due to methane emissions from anaerobic lagoons. Field measurements of emissions at large-scale dairies in the western US have also shown that lagoons and slurry systems have the highest GWP of any manure storage method, and the mean contribution from lagoons was 20 times higher than the mean contribution from manure storage (Owen and Silver, 2015). Lagoons have a much higher methane conversion factor than solid storage, making liquid storage in lagoons one of the key contrib- utors to GWP. A GHG assessment of milk production in the US found that

regions with a high prevalence of anaerobic lagoons had a higher contribution from manure management (Thoma et al., 2012). In addi- tion, a survey of Wisconsin dairies performed found that larger dairies tended to handle liquid manure, which had a greater contribution to GHG emissions than solid storage (Aguirre-Villegas and Larson, 2017).

Replacing liquid manure storage with AD has been shown to reduce GWP. In a study covering different manure processing pathways for Wisconsin dairy farms, it was determined that AD reduced GWP by 48% relative to a base case that included lagoon storage (Aguirre-Villegas et al., 2014). The AD scenario included benefits from avoided fossil CO2 when grid electricity was replaced by biogas electricity. In CA, a study conducted for CARB showed that all types of AD systems, including lagoon digesters, resulted in GHG reductions compared to conventional storage in a lagoon (Kaffka et al., 2016). AD is one of the most promising methods to reach emission reduction targets set by Senate Bill 1383 in CA, and current AD projects funded through the Dairy Digester Research and Development Program are expected to provide 2.0 MMT CO2-eq. in emission reductions per year (CARB, 2022a). However, more progress is needed to meet emission targets. With current AD systems and projected cow population reduction, only half of the targeted emission reduction will be met in 2030 (CARB, 2022a). CARB projections show additional AD systems and alternative manure management strategies are needed to meet the targeted reductions. Alternatively, technologies with greater GWP reduction potential could help meet the targeted emissions for dairy farms.

In the NEWIR scenarios, the type of algae has a major effect on the total GWP. Cultivation of S. maxima has a high contribution to GWP, bringing the NEWIR-max scenario to the highest gross total of 479 + 13.4 kg CO2-eq., higher than the baseline scenario total. Production of sodium carbonate and sodium bicarbonate to maintain a pH of 9.5 in the S. maxima pond is the dominant contributor at 90% of the NEWIR-max gross total. Manufacturing these chemicals has a high GWP, primarily due to fossil energy sources used in the production process (US LCI, 2012; Wernet et al., 2016). In the NEWIR-max scenario, the other unit processes have a relatively small contribution. HTC accounts for only 2.0% (9.5 kg CO2-eq.) of the gross total; most of the HTC impact comes from combustion of natural gas to heat the reactor. Short-term storage of manure prior to HTC contributes 2.5%. Synthetic fertilizer, which is used to grow corn and wheat because the pond effluent cannot be land applied due to presence of the chemical buffers, contributes 1.6% of the total GWP.

In the other three NEWIR scenarios, GWP is substantially lower than NEWIR-max as well as the baseline and AD scenarios. NEWIR-rein, NEWIR-vulg, and NEWIR-obliq have similar gross total GWPs of 44.2 + 3.0, 42.2 + 2.9, and 42.2 + 3.6 kg CO2-eq., respectively. The algae species grown in these scenarios do not require pH adjustment of HAP, eliminating the need for chemical additions. Algae cultivation still contributes to GWP through electricity use for paddlewheel mixing in the raceway ponds, biomass harvesting, and pumping, but the contri- bution is much smaller with 25-26% of the gross total GWP in each scenario. HTC and short-term storage have the same magnitude GWP as in NEWIR-max, but the relative contribution is higher due to the lower gross total; HTC contributes 21.4-22.4% and storage contributes 27.4-28.7% of the total depending on the scenario. With respect to land application, the pond effluents from cultivation of C. reinhardtii, C. vul- garis, and S. obliquus can all be used for irrigation because there are no buffer salts added. Nutrients that are not removed by the algae are therefore available to plants as fertilizer, reducing demand for synthetic fertilizer relative to NEWIR-max. The amount of synthetic fertilizer varies depending on the amount of N and P remaining in the pond effluent. C. reinhardtii had the highest nutrient removal and therefore requires the most synthetic fertilizer to reach crop demands, while S. obliquus had the lowest removal. Fertilizer contributed 11.3% of the NEWIR-rein total, 9.4% of the NEWIR-vulg total, and 9.8% of the NEWIR-obliq total.
In each NEWIR scenario, there are also benefits to GWP through

resource recovery. Electricity produced from coal is replaced by elec- tricity from hydrochar and soybean in the cow diet is replaced with algae-based cattle feed. The amount of hydrochar produced is the same in all four NEWIR scenarios, providing a benefit of 71.4 kg CO2-eq. (Fig. 4). Much greater energy recovery is possible through HTC and hydrochar than through AD and biogas, as seen by the difference in GWP offsets. These results are supported by experimental studies where HTC of dairy manure recovers more than twice the amount of energy as AD (Marin-Batista et al., 2020). Other studies have identified GWP benefits when hydrochar replaces coal for electricity production (Benavente et al., 2017; Berge et al., 2015). In a study evaluating HTC for food waste processing, using hydrochar for electricity production improved GWP relative to coal and the average US energy mix (Berge et al., 2015).

The amount of soybean that can be replaced with algae is dependent on the algae species since each species has unique biomass productivity and protein content when grown in HAP. Although algae cultivation is intended for nutrient recovery, it provides benefits to GWP by avoiding conventional soybean production. S. maxima has the highest biomass productivity and second highest protein content, resulting in the greatest soybean offset of 134 kg CO2-eq. avoided per functional unit. C. reinhardtii has the second highest productivity and highest protein content, providing a soybean offset of 110 kg CO2-eq. C. vulgaris and S. obliquus had lower productivities and protein contents, resulting in soybean offsets of 56.2 and 37.8 kg CO2-eq., respectively.

When avoided products are considered, the lowest net total GWP is achieved in the NEWIR-rein scenario. This scenario includes benefits from both recovered energy and nutrients with the lowest total inputs (i. e., no chemicals required for algae cultivation) resulting in net negative GWP of -137 kg CO2-eq. per functional unit. NEWIR-vulg and NEWIR- obliq also result in net negative GWP with -84.9 and -67.0 kg CO2-eq., respectively. While the inputs for these three scenarios are very similar, C. reinhartii achieves greater biomass productivity, resulting in greater offsets through avoided soybean. The net total for NEWIR-max is significantly higher at 275 kg CO2-eq.

The net totals for AD and all NEWIR scenarios are lower than the baseline scenario, indicating that resource recovery improves GHG emissions relative to conventional manure management practices. The best system between AD and NEWIR depends on the algae species selected in NEWIR. AD has a much lower net total than NEWIR-max (63.0 VS 275 kg CO2-eq.), but NEWIR-rein, NEWIR-vulg, and NEWIR- obliq all perform better than AD by a substantial margin. These results indicate that integrated energy and nutrient recovery through HTC and algae cultivation can provide greater benefits to GWP than AD with the right process selection.

When avoided products are considered, the lowest net total GWP is achieved in the NEWIR-rein scenario. This scenario includes benefits from both recovered energy and nutrients with the lowest total inputs (i. e., no chemicals required for algae cultivation) resulting in net negative GWP of -137 kg CO2-eq. per functional unit. NEWIR-vulg and NEWIR- obliq also result in net negative GWP with -84.9 and -67.0 kg CO2-eq., respectively. While the inputs for these three scenarios are very similar, C. reinhartii achieves greater biomass productivity, resulting in greater offsets through avoided soybean. The net total for NEWIR-max is significantly higher at 275 kg CO2-eq.

The net totals for AD and all NEWIR scenarios are lower than the baseline scenario, indicating that resource recovery improves GHG emissions relative to conventional manure management practices. The best system between AD and NEWIR depends on the algae species selected in NEWIR. AD has a much lower net total than NEWIR-max (63.0 VS 275 kg CO2-eq.), but NEWIR-rein, NEWIR-vulg, and NEWIR- obliq all perform better than AD by a substantial margin. These results indicate that integrated energy and nutrient recovery through HTC and algae cultivation can provide greater benefits to GWP than AD with the right process selection.
41: Of the six scenarios evaluated, the baseline scenario representing conventional manure management practices has the highest net total GWP of 361 + 18.0 kg CO2-eq. per 1000 kg manure functional unit (Fig. 4). Manure storage is the largest contributor to the total baseline GWP at 90.8%, with emissions from the anaerobic lagoon, which are dominated by methane, the majority contributing 87% of the total baseline GWP. Thus, reducing manure storage in anaerobic conditions has the greatest potential for reducing GWP. Solid storage contributes only 3.3% of the total GWP. Land application, which includes nitrous oxide emissions from manure application as well as production and combustion of diesel fuel to operate machinery, and collection each have a smaller contribution of 7.4% and 1.8%, respectively. Collection in- cludes GHG emissions from manure, fuel use, and operation of the RO system to provide clean water for the milk processing equipment.

Compared to the baseline, AD substantially reduces GWP from manure management with a gross total of 73.4 + 5.7 kg CO2-eq. When the benefits of energy recovery from biogas are considered, the net total is reduced to 63.0 + 7.0 kg CO2-eq. The dominant contributor is land application (40.9%), followed by the digestion process (25.3%) and storage (25.0%). Storage emissions come from scraped solid manure and separated solids, which do not enter the digester. Lagoon emissions are avoided because the lagoon digester is covered, reducing the overall GWP significantly relative to the baseline. The AD process emissions include leaks and inefficiencies in the digester and biogas processing equipment, as well as emissions from digestate storage that account for about half of the AD unit process GWP or 12.9% of the gross total for the scenario. This GWP primarily comes from methane, which is emitted due to residual VS not consumed during digestion, as well as nitrous oxide. Reducing the length of digestate storage or covering the digestate storage pond, as done in some biogas projects (Summers and Williams, 2013), can help reduce the emissions associated with this step. In land application, emissions are higher than the baseline due to increased nitrous oxide emissions (22.6 CO2-eq. in AD versus 19.4 kg CO2-eq. in baseline). During AD, ammonia emissions are reduced compared to the uncovered lagoon in the baseline, leading to a higher N content in the
[ ] Collection|[ ] HTC|[ ] Algae Cultivation [ ] Storage|[ ] Grid Electricity Offset|[ ] Coal Offset [ ] Soybean Offset [ ] AD|[ ] Land Application|[ ] Net Total
Fig. 4. Results for global warming potential (GWP) of each scenario. Error bars represent the standard deviation obtained from uncertainty analysis.
digestate as compared to the lagoon effluent resulting in greater N emissions as nitrous oxide and ammonia during both digestate storage and land application.

The AD scenario also provides a benefit to GWP in the form of an avoided product - CA grid electricity that has been replaced by elec- tricity from biogas. 10.4 kg CO2-eq. is avoided through use of biogas for electricity production instead of the grid (Fig. 4).

The results for both the AD and baseline scenarios demonstrate the effectiveness of AD as a GHG mitigation method, consistent with the literature (Naranjo et al., 2020; Owen and Silver, 2015; Aguirre-Villegas and Larson, 2017; Thoma et al., 2012). Many studies report a high contribution to GWP from manure storage, specifically methane from lagoons. Naranjo et al. (2020) conducted an LCA comparing the envi- ronmental impacts of milk production in CA between 1964 and 2014 to determine the effects of different farm management practices. In the 2014 models, manure management accounted for 40.5-41.1% of the total GWP associated with milk production in CA, primarily due to methane emissions from anaerobic lagoons. Field measurements of emissions at large-scale dairies in the western US have also shown that lagoons and slurry systems have the highest GWP of any manure storage method, and the mean contribution from lagoons was 20 times higher than the mean contribution from manure storage (Owen and Silver, 2015). Lagoons have a much higher methane conversion factor than solid storage, making liquid storage in lagoons one of the key contrib- utors to GWP. A GHG assessment of milk production in the US found that

regions with a high prevalence of anaerobic lagoons had a higher contribution from manure management (Thoma et al., 2012). In addi- tion, a survey of Wisconsin dairies performed found that larger dairies tended to handle liquid manure, which had a greater contribution to GHG emissions than solid storage (Aguirre-Villegas and Larson, 2017).

Replacing liquid manure storage with AD has been shown to reduce GWP. In a study covering different manure processing pathways for Wisconsin dairy farms, it was determined that AD reduced GWP by 48% relative to a base case that included lagoon storage (Aguirre-Villegas et al., 2014). The AD scenario included benefits from avoided fossil CO2 when grid electricity was replaced by biogas electricity. In CA, a study conducted for CARB showed that all types of AD systems, including lagoon digesters, resulted in GHG reductions compared to conventional storage in a lagoon (Kaffka et al., 2016). AD is one of the most promising methods to reach emission reduction targets set by Senate Bill 1383 in CA, and current AD projects funded through the Dairy Digester Research and Development Program are expected to provide 2.0 MMT CO2-eq. in emission reductions per year (CARB, 2022a). However, more progress is needed to meet emission targets. With current AD systems and projected cow population reduction, only half of the targeted emission reduction will be met in 2030 (CARB, 2022a). CARB projections show additional AD systems and alternative manure management strategies are needed to meet the targeted reductions. Alternatively, technologies with greater GWP reduction potential could help meet the targeted emissions for dairy farms.

In the NEWIR scenarios, the type of algae has a major effect on the total GWP. Cultivation of S. maxima has a high contribution to GWP, bringing the NEWIR-max scenario to the highest gross total of 479 + 13.4 kg CO2-eq., higher than the baseline scenario total. Production of sodium carbonate and sodium bicarbonate to maintain a pH of 9.5 in the S. maxima pond is the dominant contributor at 90% of the NEWIR-max gross total. Manufacturing these chemicals has a high GWP, primarily due to fossil energy sources used in the production process (US LCI, 2012; Wernet et al., 2016). In the NEWIR-max scenario, the other unit processes have a relatively small contribution. HTC accounts for only 2.0% (9.5 kg CO2-eq.) of the gross total; most of the HTC impact comes from combustion of natural gas to heat the reactor. Short-term storage of manure prior to HTC contributes 2.5%. Synthetic fertilizer, which is used to grow corn and wheat because the pond effluent cannot be land applied due to presence of the chemical buffers, contributes 1.6% of the total GWP.

In the other three NEWIR scenarios, GWP is substantially lower than NEWIR-max as well as the baseline and AD scenarios. NEWIR-rein, NEWIR-vulg, and NEWIR-obliq have similar gross total GWPs of 44.2 + 3.0, 42.2 + 2.9, and 42.2 + 3.6 kg CO2-eq., respectively. The algae species grown in these scenarios do not require pH adjustment of HAP, eliminating the need for chemical additions. Algae cultivation still contributes to GWP through electricity use for paddlewheel mixing in the raceway ponds, biomass harvesting, and pumping, but the contri- bution is much smaller with 25-26% of the gross total GWP in each scenario. HTC and short-term storage have the same magnitude GWP as in NEWIR-max, but the relative contribution is higher due to the lower gross total; HTC contributes 21.4-22.4% and storage contributes 27.4-28.7% of the total depending on the scenario. With respect to land application, the pond effluents from cultivation of C. reinhardtii, C. vul- garis, and S. obliquus can all be used for irrigation because there are no buffer salts added. Nutrients that are not removed by the algae are therefore available to plants as fertilizer, reducing demand for synthetic fertilizer relative to NEWIR-max. The amount of synthetic fertilizer varies depending on the amount of N and P remaining in the pond effluent. C. reinhardtii had the highest nutrient removal and therefore requires the most synthetic fertilizer to reach crop demands, while S. obliquus had the lowest removal. Fertilizer contributed 11.3% of the NEWIR-rein total, 9.4% of the NEWIR-vulg total, and 9.8% of the NEWIR-obliq total.
In each NEWIR scenario, there are also benefits to GWP through

resource recovery. Electricity produced from coal is replaced by elec- tricity from hydrochar and soybean in the cow diet is replaced with algae-based cattle feed. The amount of hydrochar produced is the same in all four NEWIR scenarios, providing a benefit of 71.4 kg CO2-eq. (Fig. 4). Much greater energy recovery is possible through HTC and hydrochar than through AD and biogas, as seen by the difference in GWP offsets. These results are supported by experimental studies where HTC of dairy manure recovers more than twice the amount of energy as AD (Marin-Batista et al., 2020). Other studies have identified GWP benefits when hydrochar replaces coal for electricity production (Benavente et al., 2017; Berge et al., 2015). In a study evaluating HTC for food waste processing, using hydrochar for electricity production improved GWP relative to coal and the average US energy mix (Berge et al., 2015).

The amount of soybean that can be replaced with algae is dependent on the algae species since each species has unique biomass productivity and protein content when grown in HAP. Although algae cultivation is intended for nutrient recovery, it provides benefits to GWP by avoiding conventional soybean production. S. maxima has the highest biomass productivity and second highest protein content, resulting in the greatest soybean offset of 134 kg CO2-eq. avoided per functional unit. C. reinhardtii has the second highest productivity and highest protein content, providing a soybean offset of 110 kg CO2-eq. C. vulgaris and S. obliquus had lower productivities and protein contents, resulting in soybean offsets of 56.2 and 37.8 kg CO2-eq., respectively.

When avoided products are considered, the lowest net total GWP is achieved in the NEWIR-rein scenario. This scenario includes benefits from both recovered energy and nutrients with the lowest total inputs (i. e., no chemicals required for algae cultivation) resulting in net negative GWP of -137 kg CO2-eq. per functional unit. NEWIR-vulg and NEWIR- obliq also result in net negative GWP with -84.9 and -67.0 kg CO2-eq., respectively. While the inputs for these three scenarios are very similar, C. reinhartii achieves greater biomass productivity, resulting in greater offsets through avoided soybean. The net total for NEWIR-max is significantly higher at 275 kg CO2-eq.

The net totals for AD and all NEWIR scenarios are lower than the baseline scenario, indicating that resource recovery improves GHG emissions relative to conventional manure management practices. The best system between AD and NEWIR depends on the algae species selected in NEWIR. AD has a much lower net total than NEWIR-max (63.0 VS 275 kg CO2-eq.), but NEWIR-rein, NEWIR-vulg, and NEWIR- obliq all perform better than AD by a substantial margin. These results indicate that integrated energy and nutrient recovery through HTC and algae cultivation can provide greater benefits to GWP than AD with the right process selection.

These results are supported by experimental studies where HTC of dairy manure recovers more than twice the amount of energy as AD (Marin-Batista et al., 2020). Other studies have identified GWP benefits when hydrochar replaces coal for electricity production (Benavente et al., 2017; Berge et al., 2015). In a study evaluating HTC for food waste processing, using hydrochar for electricity production improved GWP relative to coal and the average US energy mix (Berge et al., 2015).

The amount of soybean that can be replaced with algae is dependent on the algae species since each species has unique biomass productivity and protein content when grown in HAP. Although algae cultivation is intended for nutrient recovery, it provides benefits to GWP by avoiding conventional soybean production. S. maxima has the highest biomass productivity and second highest protein content, resulting in the greatest soybean offset of 134 kg CO2-eq. avoided per functional unit. C. reinhardtii has the second highest productivity and highest protein content, providing a soybean offset of 110 kg CO2-eq. C. vulgaris and S. obliquus had lower productivities and protein contents, resulting in soybean offsets of 56.2 and 37.8 kg CO2-eq., respectively.

When avoided products are considered, the lowest net total GWP is achieved in the NEWIR-rein scenario. This scenario includes benefits from both recovered energy and nutrients with the lowest total inputs (i. e., no chemicals required for algae cultivation) resulting in net negative GWP of -137 kg CO2-eq. per functional unit. NEWIR-vulg and NEWIR- obliq also result in net negative GWP with -84.9 and -67.0 kg CO2-eq., respectively. While the inputs for these three scenarios are very similar, C. reinhartii achieves greater biomass productivity, resulting in greater offsets through avoided soybean. The net total for NEWIR-max is significantly higher at 275 kg CO2-eq.

The net totals for AD and all NEWIR scenarios are lower than the baseline scenario, indicating that resource recovery improves GHG emissions relative to conventional manure management practices. The best system between AD and NEWIR depends on the algae species selected in NEWIR. AD has a much lower net total than NEWIR-max (63.0 VS 275 kg CO2-eq.), but NEWIR-rein, NEWIR-vulg, and NEWIR- obliq all perform better than AD by a substantial margin. These results indicate that integrated energy and nutrient recovery through HTC and algae cultivation can provide greater benefits to GWP than AD with the right process selection.
44: Of the six scenarios evaluated, the baseline scenario representing conventional manure management practices has the highest net total GWP of 361 + 18.0 kg CO2-eq. per 1000 kg manure functional unit (Fig. 4). Manure storage is the largest contributor to the total baseline GWP at 90.8%, with emissions from the anaerobic lagoon, which are dominated by methane, the majority contributing 87% of the total baseline GWP. Thus, reducing manure storage in anaerobic conditions has the greatest potential for reducing GWP. Solid storage contributes only 3.3% of the total GWP. Land application, which includes nitrous oxide emissions from manure application as well as production and combustion of diesel fuel to operate machinery, and collection each have a smaller contribution of 7.4% and 1.8%, respectively. Collection in- cludes GHG emissions from manure, fuel use, and operation of the RO system to provide clean water for the milk processing equipment.

Compared to the baseline, AD substantially reduces GWP from manure management with a gross total of 73.4 + 5.7 kg CO2-eq. When the benefits of energy recovery from biogas are considered, the net total is reduced to 63.0 + 7.0 kg CO2-eq. The dominant contributor is land application (40.9%), followed by the digestion process (25.3%) and storage (25.0%). Storage emissions come from scraped solid manure and separated solids, which do not enter the digester. Lagoon emissions are avoided because the lagoon digester is covered, reducing the overall GWP significantly relative to the baseline. The AD process emissions include leaks and inefficiencies in the digester and biogas processing equipment, as well as emissions from digestate storage that account for about half of the AD unit process GWP or 12.9% of the gross total for the scenario. This GWP primarily comes from methane, which is emitted due to residual VS not consumed during digestion, as well as nitrous oxide. Reducing the length of digestate storage or covering the digestate storage pond, as done in some biogas projects (Summers and Williams, 2013), can help reduce the emissions associated with this step. In land application, emissions are higher than the baseline due to increased nitrous oxide emissions (22.6 CO2-eq. in AD versus 19.4 kg CO2-eq. in baseline). During AD, ammonia emissions are reduced compared to the uncovered lagoon in the baseline, leading to a higher N content in the
[ ] Collection|[ ] HTC|[ ] Algae Cultivation [ ] Storage|[ ] Grid Electricity Offset|[ ] Coal Offset [ ] Soybean Offset [ ] AD|[ ] Land Application|[ ] Net Total
Fig. 4. Results for global warming potential (GWP) of each scenario. Error bars represent the standard deviation obtained from uncertainty analysis.
digestate as compared to the lagoon effluent resulting in greater N emissions as nitrous oxide and ammonia during both digestate storage and land application.

The AD scenario also provides a benefit to GWP in the form of an avoided product - CA grid electricity that has been replaced by elec- tricity from biogas. 10.4 kg CO2-eq. is avoided through use of biogas for electricity production instead of the grid (Fig. 4).

The results for both the AD and baseline scenarios demonstrate the effectiveness of AD as a GHG mitigation method, consistent with the literature (Naranjo et al., 2020; Owen and Silver, 2015; Aguirre-Villegas and Larson, 2017; Thoma et al., 2012). Many studies report a high contribution to GWP from manure storage, specifically methane from lagoons. Naranjo et al. (2020) conducted an LCA comparing the envi- ronmental impacts of milk production in CA between 1964 and 2014 to determine the effects of different farm management practices. In the 2014 models, manure management accounted for 40.5-41.1% of the total GWP associated with milk production in CA, primarily due to methane emissions from anaerobic lagoons. Field measurements of emissions at large-scale dairies in the western US have also shown that lagoons and slurry systems have the highest GWP of any manure storage method, and the mean contribution from lagoons was 20 times higher than the mean contribution from manure storage (Owen and Silver, 2015). Lagoons have a much higher methane conversion factor than solid storage, making liquid storage in lagoons one of the key contrib- utors to GWP. A GHG assessment of milk production in the US found that

regions with a high prevalence of anaerobic lagoons had a higher contribution from manure management (Thoma et al., 2012). In addi- tion, a survey of Wisconsin dairies performed found that larger dairies tended to handle liquid manure, which had a greater contribution to GHG emissions than solid storage (Aguirre-Villegas and Larson, 2017).

Replacing liquid manure storage with AD has been shown to reduce GWP. In a study covering different manure processing pathways for Wisconsin dairy farms, it was determined that AD reduced GWP by 48% relative to a base case that included lagoon storage (Aguirre-Villegas et al., 2014). The AD scenario included benefits from avoided fossil CO2 when grid electricity was replaced by biogas electricity. In CA, a study conducted for CARB showed that all types of AD systems, including lagoon digesters, resulted in GHG reductions compared to conventional storage in a lagoon (Kaffka et al., 2016). AD is one of the most promising methods to reach emission reduction targets set by Senate Bill 1383 in CA, and current AD projects funded through the Dairy Digester Research and Development Program are expected to provide 2.0 MMT CO2-eq. in emission reductions per year (CARB, 2022a). However, more progress is needed to meet emission targets. With current AD systems and projected cow population reduction, only half of the targeted emission reduction will be met in 2030 (CARB, 2022a). CARB projections show additional AD systems and alternative manure management strategies are needed to meet the targeted reductions. Alternatively, technologies with greater GWP reduction potential could help meet the targeted emissions for dairy farms.

In the NEWIR scenarios, the type of algae has a major effect on the total GWP. Cultivation of S. maxima has a high contribution to GWP, bringing the NEWIR-max scenario to the highest gross total of 479 + 13.4 kg CO2-eq., higher than the baseline scenario total. Production of sodium carbonate and sodium bicarbonate to maintain a pH of 9.5 in the S. maxima pond is the dominant contributor at 90% of the NEWIR-max gross total. Manufacturing these chemicals has a high GWP, primarily due to fossil energy sources used in the production process (US LCI, 2012; Wernet et al., 2016). In the NEWIR-max scenario, the other unit processes have a relatively small contribution. HTC accounts for only 2.0% (9.5 kg CO2-eq.) of the gross total; most of the HTC impact comes from combustion of natural gas to heat the reactor. Short-term storage of manure prior to HTC contributes 2.5%. Synthetic fertilizer, which is used to grow corn and wheat because the pond effluent cannot be land applied due to presence of the chemical buffers, contributes 1.6% of the total GWP.

In the other three NEWIR scenarios, GWP is substantially lower than NEWIR-max as well as the baseline and AD scenarios. NEWIR-rein, NEWIR-vulg, and NEWIR-obliq have similar gross total GWPs of 44.2 + 3.0, 42.2 + 2.9, and 42.2 + 3.6 kg CO2-eq., respectively. The algae species grown in these scenarios do not require pH adjustment of HAP, eliminating the need for chemical additions. Algae cultivation still contributes to GWP through electricity use for paddlewheel mixing in the raceway ponds, biomass harvesting, and pumping, but the contri- bution is much smaller with 25-26% of the gross total GWP in each scenario. HTC and short-term storage have the same magnitude GWP as in NEWIR-max, but the relative contribution is higher due to the lower gross total; HTC contributes 21.4-22.4% and storage contributes 27.4-28.7% of the total depending on the scenario. With respect to land application, the pond effluents from cultivation of C. reinhardtii, C. vul- garis, and S. obliquus can all be used for irrigation because there are no buffer salts added. Nutrients that are not removed by the algae are therefore available to plants as fertilizer, reducing demand for synthetic fertilizer relative to NEWIR-max. The amount of synthetic fertilizer varies depending on the amount of N and P remaining in the pond effluent. C. reinhardtii had the highest nutrient removal and therefore requires the most synthetic fertilizer to reach crop demands, while S. obliquus had the lowest removal. Fertilizer contributed 11.3% of the NEWIR-rein total, 9.4% of the NEWIR-vulg total, and 9.8% of the NEWIR-obliq total.
In each NEWIR scenario, there are also benefits to GWP through

resource recovery. Electricity produced from coal is replaced by elec- tricity from hydrochar and soybean in the cow diet is replaced with algae-based cattle feed. The amount of hydrochar produced is the same in all four NEWIR scenarios, providing a benefit of 71.4 kg CO2-eq. (Fig. 4). Much greater energy recovery is possible through HTC and hydrochar than through AD and biogas, as seen by the difference in GWP offsets. These results are supported by experimental studies where HTC of dairy manure recovers more than twice the amount of energy as AD (Marin-Batista et al., 2020). Other studies have identified GWP benefits when hydrochar replaces coal for electricity production (Benavente et al., 2017; Berge et al., 2015). Nutrients that are not removed by the algae are therefore available to plants as fertilizer, reducing demand for synthetic fertilizer relative to NEWIR-max. The amount of synthetic fertilizer varies depending on the amount of N and P remaining in the pond effluent. C. reinhardtii had the highest nutrient removal and therefore requires the most synthetic fertilizer to reach crop demands, while S. obliquus had the lowest removal. Fertilizer contributed 11.3% of the NEWIR-rein total, 9.4% of the NEWIR-vulg total, and 9.8% of the NEWIR-obliq total.
In each NEWIR scenario, there are also benefits to GWP through

resource recovery. Electricity produced from coal is replaced by elec- tricity from hydrochar and soybean in the cow diet is replaced with algae-based cattle feed. The amount of hydrochar produced is the same in all four NEWIR scenarios, providing a benefit of 71.4 kg CO2-eq. (Fig. 4). Much greater energy recovery is possible through HTC and hydrochar than through AD and biogas, as seen by the difference in GWP offsets. These results are supported by experimental studies where HTC of dairy manure recovers more than twice the amount of energy as AD (Marin-Batista et al., 2020). Other studies have identified GWP benefits when hydrochar replaces coal for electricity production (Benavente et al., 2017; Berge et al., 2015). In a study evaluating HTC for food waste processing, using hydrochar for electricity production improved GWP relative to coal and the average US energy mix (Berge et al., 2015).

The amount of soybean that can be replaced with algae is dependent on the algae species since each species has unique biomass productivity and protein content when grown in HAP. Although algae cultivation is intended for nutrient recovery, it provides benefits to GWP by avoiding conventional soybean production. S. maxima has the highest biomass productivity and second highest protein content, resulting in the greatest soybean offset of 134 kg CO2-eq. avoided per functional unit. C. reinhardtii has the second highest productivity and highest protein content, providing a soybean offset of 110 kg CO2-eq. C. vulgaris and S. obliquus had lower productivities and protein contents, resulting in soybean offsets of 56.2 and 37.8 kg CO2-eq., respectively.

When avoided products are considered, the lowest net total GWP is achieved in the NEWIR-rein scenario. This scenario includes benefits from both recovered energy and nutrients with the lowest total inputs (i. e., no chemicals required for algae cultivation) resulting in net negative GWP of -137 kg CO2-eq. per functional unit. NEWIR-vulg and NEWIR- obliq also result in net negative GWP with -84.9 and -67.0 kg CO2-eq., respectively. While the inputs for these three scenarios are very similar, C. reinhartii achieves greater biomass productivity, resulting in greater offsets through avoided soybean. The net total for NEWIR-max is significantly higher at 275 kg CO2-eq.

The net totals for AD and all NEWIR scenarios are lower than the baseline scenario, indicating that resource recovery improves GHG emissions relative to conventional manure management practices. The best system between AD and NEWIR depends on the algae species selected in NEWIR. AD has a much lower net total than NEWIR-max (63.0 VS 275 kg CO2-eq.), but NEWIR-rein, NEWIR-vulg, and NEWIR- obliq all perform better than AD by a substantial margin. These results indicate that integrated energy and nutrient recovery through HTC and algae cultivation can provide greater benefits to GWP than AD with the right process selection.
47: Eutrophication impacts caused by emissions of N and P to water are reported in the MEP and FEP categories, respectively. Conventional manure management practices primarily emit N and P to surface and groundwater during the land application stage (Fig. 5). In studies per- formed in the Central Valley of CA, most emissions of nitrate to groundwater come from land application of nutrient sources, including manure (Rosenstock et al., 2014). Leaching and surface water runoff from cow housing and manure storage can also contribute, but these emissions are minimal compared to those from land application. The MEP and FEP results follow these observed trends, with the baseline MEP and FEP coming solely from application of manure to crop fields. The baseline MEP is 0.174 + 0.007 kg N-eq. per functional unit. The N content of manure during land application is affected by volatilization in previous management stages, with ammonia and nitrous oxide lost to the atmosphere during collection and storage. The remaining N at the end of storage is applied as an organic fertilizer and subject to the N application regulations as set by the 2013 Dairy Order (R5-2013-0122, 2013). Meanwhile, P does not volatilize at any stage, so all P present in manure is available for land application. The baseline FEP is 0.262 + 0.012 kg P-eq. per functional unit.
[ ] Manure Application|[ ] Digestate Application|[ ] Algae Cultivation|[ ] Soybean Offset [ ] Pond Effluent Application|[ ] Fertilizer Application|[X] Coal Offset|[ ] Net Total
AD also results in MEP and FEP due to land application of digestate.

Fig. 5. Eutrophication potential results for A) marine eutrophication potential (MEP) and B) freshwater eutrophication potential (FEP) for the LCA scenarios. Error bars represent the standard deviation obtained from uncertainty analysis.

Relative to the baseline, AD results in slightly higher MEP (0.205 0.017 kg N-eq.) and slightly lower FEP (0.241 I 0.011 kg P-eq.). Nu- trients are conserved during the AD process (Summers and Williams, 2013), but manure handling through AD changes how much N is released through volatilization. The covered lagoon digester reduces N volatilization as ammonia and nitrous oxide, leading to a higher N content in the digestate compared to the lagoon effluent in the baseline. Some N is volatilized during digestate storage, but the amount is dependent on the duration; in this study, digestate is stored for 30 days, much less time than storage in the lagoon in the baseline. Therefore, less N is volatilized, and more is available during land application. Since the same N application limits are followed, more land area is required to spread the digestate. As a result, more N is available for leaching and runoff from the crop fields, resulting in higher MEP than the baseline scenario. Although P is not affected by volatilization or the digestion process, because the digestate is spread over a larger land area, less P is applied per unit area and therefore less FEP occurs relative to the baseline. Nutrient conservation during AD means that digestate must be managed to reduce nutrient runoff and leaching in the same manner as the baseline. AD does not provide nutrient recovery benefits on its own, although many studies have evaluated pairing AD with a nutrient re- covery method such as algae cultivation (Higgins and Kendall, 2012), struvite precipitation (Temizel-Sekeryan et al., 2020), or ion exchange (Amini et al., 2017) to capture nutrients in a more concentrated and useful form. However, none of these practices are standard on CA dairies, and the current AD methods pose similar risks to water resources as conventional practices.

In the NEWIR scenarios, gross total MEP is similar to the baseline. The same N application limits are followed in all scenarios, and in each NEWIR scenario the same land area of corn and wheat is assumed to be cultivated as in the baseline, resulting in the same MEP. However, the source of N varies, with NEWIR using synthetic fertilizer and algae pond effluent to provide nutrients to crops. In NEWIR-max, the pond effluent cannot be used for irrigation due to buffer salts, so all of the nutrients are provided by synthetic fertilizer. Manufacturing buffer chemicals also has a small contribution to the NEWIR-max scenario, bringing the gross total to 0.189 N-eq., slightly higher than the baseline but lower than AD. In the NEWIR-rein, NEWIR-vulg, and NEWIR-obliq scenarios, the total MEP is the same as the baseline (0.174 I 0.012 kg N-eq.), though the uncertainty range is larger due to uncertainties associated with the coal and soybean offsets. In NEWIR-rein, pond effluent contributes 49% of the MEP and fertilizer contributes 51%. In NEWIR-vulg and NEWIR- obliq, the algae remove less N during cultivation, so more is available for land application, with 65% and 63% of the MEP from pond effluent in each scenario, respectively, and the remainder from synthetic fertilizer.

In each of the NEWIR scenarios, replacing soybean with algae ben- efits MEP by avoiding conventional crop production. Similar to GWP, the magnitude of the avoided soybean benefit depends on the produc- tivity of the algae species. S. maxima had the greatest soybean offset (0.093 kg N-eq.), bringing the net total of NEWIR-max to 0.093 kg N-eq., the lowest of all six scenarios. The soybean offset in NEWIR-rein (0.077 kg N-eq.) is smaller than NEWIR-max, but since NEWIR-rein does not include impacts from buffer chemicals, the net total is only slightly higher than NEWIR-max at 0.095 kg N-eq. C. vulgaris and S. obliquus had lower growth and protein content, resulting in lower soybean offsets; the net totals for NEWIR-vulg and NEWIR-obliq were 0.132 and 0.145 kg N- eq., respectively. However, all four NEWIR scenarios resulted in lower MEP than both the baseline and AD, indicating that nutrient recovery reduces N emissions to water relative to conventional practices and energy recovery alone.

FEP for NEWIR follows different trends than MEP for two reasons. First, manufacturing buffer chemicals for cultivation of S. maxima in NEWIR-max has a high contribution to FEP (0.217 I 0.033 kg P-eq. or 79% of the NEWIR-max gross total) due to fossil fuel energy sources used in the manufacturing process. In the other NEWIR scenarios, these

chemicals are not used, eliminating this impact. Uncertainty is similar among the NEWIR scenarios due to the coal offset. Second, the emissions from synthetic fertilizer and pond effluent application in NEWIR are much lower than P emissions from application of manure and digestate. In the baseline and AD scenarios, application of manure or digestate was based on the N content and abiding by the N application limit. The application of P was based on the P content of the manure or digestate. Applying manure based on the N content can lead to excess application of P due to the amounts present in manure (R5-2013-0122, 2013), as seen here in FEP. In the NEWIR scenarios, it was assumed that appli- cation of N and P could be tailored to meet crop needs, reducing excess application of P relative to the baseline and AD scenarios. This leads to lower FEP from land application. In NEWIR-rein, NEWIR-vulg, and NEWIR-obliq scenarios, where land application is the only contributor to FEP, the gross total is much lower than AD and baseline (Fig. 5B).

The main benefit from avoided products in FEP comes from avoided coal. As previously discussed, fossil fuels, particularly coal, have a high FEP impact through emissions of P in the mining and extraction process (Wernet et al., 2016). Replacing coal with hydrochar avoids these im- pacts while generating the same amount of electricity; the result is an FEP benefit of 0.043 kg P-eq. in all NEWIR scenarios. Avoided soybean provides a smaller benefit, with the value depending on the species used (Fig. 5B).

Considering avoided products, the net totals of all NEWIR scenarios are lower than the baseline and AD scenarios for FEP. NEWIR-max has the highest net total of the NEWIR scenarios with 0.215 kg P-eq., close to the baseline and AD values of 0.262 and 0.241 kg P-eq., respectively. The other NEWIR scenarios have substantially lower net totals because no chemical inputs are required. These results indicate that NEWIR can greatly improve FEP relative to the baseline and AD scenarios if the right algae species are selected.

In NEWIR-vulg and NEWIR- obliq, the algae remove less N during cultivation, so more is available for land application, with 65% and 63% of the MEP from pond effluent in each scenario, respectively, and the remainder from synthetic fertilizer.

In each of the NEWIR scenarios, replacing soybean with algae ben- efits MEP by avoiding conventional crop production. Similar to GWP, the magnitude of the avoided soybean benefit depends on the produc- tivity of the algae species. S. maxima had the greatest soybean offset (0.093 kg N-eq.), bringing the net total of NEWIR-max to 0.093 kg N-eq., the lowest of all six scenarios. The soybean offset in NEWIR-rein (0.077 kg N-eq.) is smaller than NEWIR-max, but since NEWIR-rein does not include impacts from buffer chemicals, the net total is only slightly higher than NEWIR-max at 0.095 kg N-eq. C. vulgaris and S. obliquus had lower growth and protein content, resulting in lower soybean offsets; the net totals for NEWIR-vulg and NEWIR-obliq were 0.132 and 0.145 kg N- eq., respectively. However, all four NEWIR scenarios resulted in lower MEP than both the baseline and AD, indicating that nutrient recovery reduces N emissions to water relative to conventional practices and energy recovery alone.

FEP for NEWIR follows different trends than MEP for two reasons. First, manufacturing buffer chemicals for cultivation of S. maxima in NEWIR-max has a high contribution to FEP (0.217 I 0.033 kg P-eq. or 79% of the NEWIR-max gross total) due to fossil fuel energy sources used in the manufacturing process. In the other NEWIR scenarios, these

chemicals are not used, eliminating this impact. Uncertainty is similar among the NEWIR scenarios due to the coal offset. Second, the emissions from synthetic fertilizer and pond effluent application in NEWIR are much lower than P emissions from application of manure and digestate. In the baseline and AD scenarios, application of manure or digestate was based on the N content and abiding by the N application limit. The application of P was based on the P content of the manure or digestate. Applying manure based on the N content can lead to excess application of P due to the amounts present in manure (R5-2013-0122, 2013), as seen here in FEP. In the NEWIR scenarios, it was assumed that appli- cation of N and P could be tailored to meet crop needs, reducing excess application of P relative to the baseline and AD scenarios. This leads to lower FEP from land application. In NEWIR-rein, NEWIR-vulg, and NEWIR-obliq scenarios, where land application is the only contributor to FEP, the gross total is much lower than AD and baseline (Fig. 5B).

The main benefit from avoided products in FEP comes from avoided coal. As previously discussed, fossil fuels, particularly coal, have a high FEP impact through emissions of P in the mining and extraction process (Wernet et al., 2016). Replacing coal with hydrochar avoids these im- pacts while generating the same amount of electricity; the result is an FEP benefit of 0.043 kg P-eq. in all NEWIR scenarios. Avoided soybean provides a smaller benefit, with the value depending on the species used (Fig. 5B).

Considering avoided products, the net totals of all NEWIR scenarios are lower than the baseline and AD scenarios for FEP. NEWIR-max has the highest net total of the NEWIR scenarios with 0.215 kg P-eq., close to the baseline and AD values of 0.262 and 0.241 kg P-eq., respectively. The other NEWIR scenarios have substantially lower net totals because no chemical inputs are required. These results indicate that NEWIR can greatly improve FEP relative to the baseline and AD scenarios if the right algae species are selected.
51: Eutrophication impacts caused by emissions of N and P to water are reported in the MEP and FEP categories, respectively. Conventional manure management practices primarily emit N and P to surface and groundwater during the land application stage (Fig. 5). In studies per- formed in the Central Valley of CA, most emissions of nitrate to groundwater come from land application of nutrient sources, including manure (Rosenstock et al., 2014). Leaching and surface water runoff from cow housing and manure storage can also contribute, but these emissions are minimal compared to those from land application. The MEP and FEP results follow these observed trends, with the baseline MEP and FEP coming solely from application of manure to crop fields. The baseline MEP is 0.174 + 0.007 kg N-eq. per functional unit. The N content of manure during land application is affected by volatilization in previous management stages, with ammonia and nitrous oxide lost to the atmosphere during collection and storage. The remaining N at the end of storage is applied as an organic fertilizer and subject to the N application regulations as set by the 2013 Dairy Order (R5-2013-0122, 2013). Meanwhile, P does not volatilize at any stage, so all P present in manure is available for land application. The baseline FEP is 0.262 + 0.012 kg P-eq. per functional unit.

AD also results in MEP and FEP due to land application of digestate.

Fig. 5. Eutrophication potential results for A) marine eutrophication potential (MEP) and B) freshwater eutrophication potential (FEP) for the LCA scenarios. Error bars represent the standard deviation obtained from uncertainty analysis.

Relative to the baseline, AD results in slightly higher MEP (0.205 0.017 kg N-eq.) and slightly lower FEP (0.241 I 0.011 kg P-eq.). Nu- trients are conserved during the AD process (Summers and Williams, 2013), but manure handling through AD changes how much N is released through volatilization. The covered lagoon digester reduces N volatilization as ammonia and nitrous oxide, leading to a higher N content in the digestate compared to the lagoon effluent in the baseline. Some N is volatilized during digestate storage, but the amount is dependent on the duration; in this study, digestate is stored for 30 days, much less time than storage in the lagoon in the baseline. Therefore, less N is volatilized, and more is available during land application. Since the same N application limits are followed, more land area is required to spread the digestate. As a result, more N is available for leaching and runoff from the crop fields, resulting in higher MEP than the baseline scenario. Although P is not affected by volatilization or the digestion process, because the digestate is spread over a larger land area, less P is applied per unit area and therefore less FEP occurs relative to the baseline. Nutrient conservation during AD means that digestate must be managed to reduce nutrient runoff and leaching in the same manner as the baseline. AD does not provide nutrient recovery benefits on its own, although many studies have evaluated pairing AD with a nutrient re- covery method such as algae cultivation (Higgins and Kendall, 2012), struvite precipitation (Temizel-Sekeryan et al., 2020), or ion exchange (Amini et al., 2017) to capture nutrients in a more concentrated and useful form. However, none of these practices are standard on CA dairies, and the current AD methods pose similar risks to water resources as conventional practices.

In the NEWIR scenarios, gross total MEP is similar to the baseline. The same N application limits are followed in all scenarios, and in each NEWIR scenario the same land area of corn and wheat is assumed to be cultivated as in the baseline, resulting in the same MEP. However, the source of N varies, with NEWIR using synthetic fertilizer and algae pond effluent to provide nutrients to crops. In NEWIR-max, the pond effluent cannot be used for irrigation due to buffer salts, so all of the nutrients are provided by synthetic fertilizer. In NEWIR-rein, pond effluent contributes 49% of the MEP and fertilizer contributes 51%. In NEWIR-vulg and NEWIR- obliq, the algae remove less N during cultivation, so more is available for land application, with 65% and 63% of the MEP from pond effluent in each scenario, respectively, and the remainder from synthetic fertilizer.

In each of the NEWIR scenarios, replacing soybean with algae ben- efits MEP by avoiding conventional crop production. Similar to GWP, the magnitude of the avoided soybean benefit depends on the produc- tivity of the algae species. S. maxima had the greatest soybean offset (0.093 kg N-eq.), bringing the net total of NEWIR-max to 0.093 kg N-eq., the lowest of all six scenarios. The soybean offset in NEWIR-rein (0.077 kg N-eq.) is smaller than NEWIR-max, but since NEWIR-rein does not include impacts from buffer chemicals, the net total is only slightly higher than NEWIR-max at 0.095 kg N-eq. C. vulgaris and S. obliquus had lower growth and protein content, resulting in lower soybean offsets; the net totals for NEWIR-vulg and NEWIR-obliq were 0.132 and 0.145 kg N- eq., respectively. However, all four NEWIR scenarios resulted in lower MEP than both the baseline and AD, indicating that nutrient recovery reduces N emissions to water relative to conventional practices and energy recovery alone.

FEP for NEWIR follows different trends than MEP for two reasons. First, manufacturing buffer chemicals for cultivation of S. maxima in NEWIR-max has a high contribution to FEP (0.217 I 0.033 kg P-eq. or 79% of the NEWIR-max gross total) due to fossil fuel energy sources used in the manufacturing process. In the other NEWIR scenarios, these

chemicals are not used, eliminating this impact. Uncertainty is similar among the NEWIR scenarios due to the coal offset. Second, the emissions from synthetic fertilizer and pond effluent application in NEWIR are much lower than P emissions from application of manure and digestate. In the baseline and AD scenarios, application of manure or digestate was based on the N content and abiding by the N application limit. The application of P was based on the P content of the manure or digestate. Applying manure based on the N content can lead to excess application of P due to the amounts present in manure (R5-2013-0122, 2013), as seen here in FEP. In the NEWIR scenarios, it was assumed that appli- cation of N and P could be tailored to meet crop needs, reducing excess application of P relative to the baseline and AD scenarios. This leads to lower FEP from land application. In NEWIR-rein, NEWIR-vulg, and NEWIR-obliq scenarios, where land application is the only contributor to FEP, the gross total is much lower than AD and baseline (Fig. 5B).

The main benefit from avoided products in FEP comes from avoided coal. As previously discussed, fossil fuels, particularly coal, have a high FEP impact through emissions of P in the mining and extraction process (Wernet et al., 2016). Replacing coal with hydrochar avoids these im- pacts while generating the same amount of electricity; the result is an FEP benefit of 0.043 kg P-eq. in all NEWIR scenarios. Avoided soybean provides a smaller benefit, with the value depending on the species used (Fig. 5B).

Considering avoided products, the net totals of all NEWIR scenarios are lower than the baseline and AD scenarios for FEP. NEWIR-max has the highest net total of the NEWIR scenarios with 0.215 kg P-eq., close to the baseline and AD values of 0.262 and 0.241 kg P-eq., respectively. The other NEWIR scenarios have substantially lower net totals because no chemical inputs are required. These results indicate that NEWIR can greatly improve FEP relative to the baseline and AD scenarios if the right algae species are selected.
55: Dairy farms use water for a variety of on-farm processes, including irrigation of crops, milk parlor and udder hygiene, drinking water for the cattle, and cattle cooling during summer months (Meyer et al., 2006; Naranjo et al., 2020). In a typical farm, the dominant contributor to water use is feed production (Rotz et al., 2021). Fresh water use for manure management is relatively low, even in flush-based collection systems, because dairies typically use recycled lagoon water to flush manure from the barns (Meyer al., 2011). In the baseline scenario, the only water included for manure management was fresh water used to flush the milk parlor (86.5% of total) and water treated by RO for cleaning the milk equipment (10.5% of total) (Fig. 6). All other on-farm processes requiring fresh water were unrelated to manure management and excluded from the system boundary.

In the AD scenario, the same collection practices and RO treatment occur, resulting in the same gross total as the baseline of 1.93 1 per functional unit. The avoided grid electricity in the AD scenario provides a minimal benefit of 0.09 m³, bringing the net total to 1.84 + just below the baseline scenario; thus, AD does not significantly change WC relative to baseline practices. Greater uncertainty occurs in the AD scenario because of the grid electricity offset.

In the NEWIR scenarios, the WC for collection is required, but the RO treatment is replaced by MD, removing 0.20 m³ in each scenario. WC is predominantly affected by the algae species and whether the pond effluent can be used for irrigation. HAP must be diluted to 5% by volume to support growth of algae, leading to a high freshwater input (24.2 for all four scenarios). In the NEWIR-rein, NEWIR-vulg, and NEWIR- obliq scenarios, most of the pond effluent (95%) can be used for irri- gation after algae cultivation. The only water lost during the process is evaporation from the ponds, water removed for the MD process, and water removed with the algae biomass during harvesting. The net total in these three scenarios is higher than the baseline (3.49-3.50 compared to
Fig. 6. Results for water consumption (WC) in each scenario. Error bars represent the standard deviation obtained from uncertainty analysis.

In NEWIR-max, the addition of buffer salts means the pond effluent cannot be applied to fields,. While MD or other membrane treatment processes could remove salts, cleaning the entire pond volume would be energy-intensive and infeasible with waste heat from HTC. Further investigation of alternate treatment methods was beyond the scope of this work. In addition, manufacturing buffer chemicals also contributes to WC, leading to a total WC for algae cultivation of 42.2 + 6.84 m³. Uncertainty is much higher in this scenario due to the buffer chemicals. Despite the high productivity of S. maxima and benefits in other cate- gories, this species is not a feasible solution to manure management challenges due to its high WC.

Avoided products provide little benefit in the WC category. Though MD recovers high quality water, the quantity is too small to meaning- fully offset total water use. Soybeans are typically cultivated in the Midwest or southeast United States and imported to California; there is very little water offset associated with avoided soybeans because these regions typically do not require large amounts of irrigation.

59: Manure from dairy operations is a major contributor to GHG emis- sions, particularly methane, and is also a notable contributor of non- point source N and P contamination of surface and groundwater. While various emission reduction targets and regulations have been enacted in an effort to address these environmental issues, more prog- ress is needed (Senate Bill 1383, 2016; CARB, 2022a; R5-2013-0122, 2013). Currently, the cost consumers pay for dairy products does not reflect the monetary cost of environmental damages from manure,

which is described as an external cost to the market (Phaneuf and Requate, 2016). Markets will fail to bring about an efficient allocation of goods and result in excess environmental costs unless external costs are internalized into the markets through policies that induce producers and consumers to bear all costs of production. Such policies typically generate incentives for producers to reduce external costs through adoption of technical methods to reduce pollution. Policy inducements often include economic instruments such as fees per unit effluent, overall caps imposed on total allowable damages with tradeable permits that allow credits to be bought and sold, and technical standards for production methods that reduce emissions (Phaneuf and Requate, 2016; Nennich et al., 2005). The basis for any form of environmental policy, however, rests on the ability to measure or model the quantity of pollutant emitted per unit product. In the case of increasing concern with environmental damages from dairy production, precise measure- ments of N, P, and GHG emissions are needed. The inventory data and results of an LCA applied to various manure management technologies are valuable in providing the basis for the policy to incorporate envi- ronmental costs into market prices, supporting a more sustainable future for dairy production.

Waste-to-energy technologies present an opportunity to co-produce energy that would otherwise be released as GHG emissions with mar- ket values for carbon credits to potentially offset the cost to producer who adopts the technology. LCA shows that energy recovery through AD or HTC reduces GHG emissions associated with manure management. In CA, biomethane from AD can generate carbon credits through the Low Carbon Fuel Standard (LCFS), which is intended to reduce the carbon footprint of the transportation sector (CARB, 2022a); thus, LCFS credits help make AD more financially feasible (Lee and Sumner, 2018) Although hydrochar would not be eligible for the LCFS because it is not a transportation fuel, other programs may incentivize electricity produc- tion from hydrochar. Renewable Energy Certificates (RECs) awarded for electricity production from renewable sources may be sold to generate revenue. More research is needed to evaluate the role of HTC for GHG reduction in market-based policies.

In contrast to GHG emissions, environmental damages and societal costs of surface and ground water pollution from manure are more locally felt. Under the Clean Water Act, nutrients that contribute to water quality problems from farms is considered as non-point source pollution with jurisdiction generally left to the states (Shortle, 2017). State policies are typically based on education programs, limited cost sharing as financial assistance, and voluntary adoption of manure management methods to reduce run-off of N and P (Shortle, 2017). Is- sues have arisen particularly in the case of waterways that cross state borders, resulting in inconsistent regulation of nutrient pollutants from non-point sources. In CA, dairies are required to report all N application to fields and keep application below 1.4 times the N harvested with the crop as part of required nutrient management plans (R5-0122-2013, 2013). However, historical overapplication of manure nutrients has resulted in widespread N contamination of groundwater in the state (Harter et al., 2012), and the social cost of these non-point source pol- lutants is passed on to municipalities that must pay for the surface or groundwater to be treated prior to use as drinking water (Condos, 2022; Harter et al., 2012).

The best strategy to mitigate nutrient pollution will depend on the location where contamination occurs. In contrast to the global benefits of reducing GHG contributions, local conditions will dictate the most beneficial nutrient runoff reduction strategies. Generally, applying mitigation technologies on farms in areas where local water supplies have high nutrient contamination will be most effective. It may also be more cost-effective for multiple smaller farms in areas of high pollution to use a centrally located manure management technology that would reduce overall nutrient-loading, rather than imposing requirements only on larger farms that can alone achieve the necessary scale for these technologies. The majority of existing programs that encourage farmers to mitigate nonpoint source pollution promote projects such as riparian

buffers and cover crops that absorb N and P to reduce runoff (Meta- xoglou and Smith, 2022). Nutrient recovery technologies effectively create point sources of pollution and offer opportunities for farms to achieve more readily measured reductions, allowing them to participate in cap-and-trade markets. An example of this model can be found in Connecticut where all municipal wastewater treatment plants partici- pate in an N trading market that functions similarly to the carbon markets previously discussed (Powers, 2006). A key difference, how- ever, in N trading markets is that the location of the polluter plays a larger role due to the localized effects of N pollution. The Connecticut market accounts for location by weighting the credits depending on the polluter location and proximity to affected watersheds. Given the nutrient management improvements shown by NEWIR, further research is needed to explore nutrient trading in conjunction with resource re- covery technologies, particularly in areas with high N pollution. LCA and inventory data collection can provide quantitative information related to nutrient emissions to help inform possible policies and determine the effectiveness of markets.

The basis for any form of environmental policy, however, rests on the ability to measure or model the quantity of pollutant emitted per unit product. In the case of increasing concern with environmental damages from dairy production, precise measure- ments of N, P, and GHG emissions are needed. The inventory data and results of an LCA applied to various manure management technologies are valuable in providing the basis for the policy to incorporate envi- ronmental costs into market prices, supporting a more sustainable future for dairy production.

Waste-to-energy technologies present an opportunity to co-produce energy that would otherwise be released as GHG emissions with mar- ket values for carbon credits to potentially offset the cost to producer who adopts the technology. LCA shows that energy recovery through AD or HTC reduces GHG emissions associated with manure management. In CA, biomethane from AD can generate carbon credits through the Low Carbon Fuel Standard (LCFS), which is intended to reduce the carbon footprint of the transportation sector (CARB, 2022a); thus, LCFS credits help make AD more financially feasible (Lee and Sumner, 2018) Although hydrochar would not be eligible for the LCFS because it is not a transportation fuel, other programs may incentivize electricity produc- tion from hydrochar. Renewable Energy Certificates (RECs) awarded for electricity production from renewable sources may be sold to generate revenue. More research is needed to evaluate the role of HTC for GHG reduction in market-based policies.

In contrast to GHG emissions, environmental damages and societal costs of surface and ground water pollution from manure are more locally felt. Under the Clean Water Act, nutrients that contribute to water quality problems from farms is considered as non-point source pollution with jurisdiction generally left to the states (Shortle, 2017). State policies are typically based on education programs, limited cost sharing as financial assistance, and voluntary adoption of manure management methods to reduce run-off of N and P (Shortle, 2017). Is- sues have arisen particularly in the case of waterways that cross state borders, resulting in inconsistent regulation of nutrient pollutants from non-point sources. In CA, dairies are required to report all N application to fields and keep application below 1.4 times the N harvested with the crop as part of required nutrient management plans (R5-0122-2013, 2013). However, historical overapplication of manure nutrients has resulted in widespread N contamination of groundwater in the state (Harter et al., 2012), and the social cost of these non-point source pol- lutants is passed on to municipalities that must pay for the surface or groundwater to be treated prior to use as drinking water (Condos, 2022; Harter et al., 2012).

The best strategy to mitigate nutrient pollution will depend on the location where contamination occurs. In contrast to the global benefits of reducing GHG contributions, local conditions will dictate the most beneficial nutrient runoff reduction strategies. Generally, applying mitigation technologies on farms in areas where local water supplies have high nutrient contamination will be most effective. It may also be more cost-effective for multiple smaller farms in areas of high pollution to use a centrally located manure management technology that would reduce overall nutrient-loading, rather than imposing requirements only on larger farms that can alone achieve the necessary scale for these technologies. The majority of existing programs that encourage farmers to mitigate nonpoint source pollution promote projects such as riparian

buffers and cover crops that absorb N and P to reduce runoff (Meta- xoglou and Smith, 2022). Nutrient recovery technologies effectively create point sources of pollution and offer opportunities for farms to achieve more readily measured reductions, allowing them to participate in cap-and-trade markets. An example of this model can be found in Connecticut where all municipal wastewater treatment plants partici- pate in an N trading market that functions similarly to the carbon markets previously discussed (Powers, 2006). A key difference, how- ever, in N trading markets is that the location of the polluter plays a larger role due to the localized effects of N pollution. The Connecticut market accounts for location by weighting the credits depending on the polluter location and proximity to affected watersheds. Given the nutrient management improvements shown by NEWIR, further research is needed to explore nutrient trading in conjunction with resource re- covery technologies, particularly in areas with high N pollution. LCA and inventory data collection can provide quantitative information related to nutrient emissions to help inform possible policies and determine the effectiveness of markets.
63: Manure from dairy operations is a major contributor to GHG emis- sions, particularly methane, and is also a notable contributor of non- point source N and P contamination of surface and groundwater. 65: NEWIR manure is a promising treatment method to address the negative environmental impacts associated with dairy manure man- agement and recover valuable energy, nutrients, and water. The envi- ronmental performance depends primarily on the type of algae species being cultivated. NEWIR-rein with C. reinhardtii performs the best out of the NEWIR scenarios. Impacts are improved relative to conventional practices in three of four environmental indicators measured, with the greatest improvement in GWP. Water consumption is increased relative to conventional practices for NEWIR-rein, NEWIR-vulg, and NEWIR- obliq. NEWIR-max has the highest impacts due to chemical inputs required for cultivation of S. maxima. With the right choice in algae species, NEWIR can improve both GHG emissions and nutrient emissions to water relative to conventional practices and AD. NEWIR provides greater energy recovery potential than AD and improves nutrient man- agement. Energy recovery through both NEWIR and AD is incentivized by existing programs for renewable energy production, though policies for nutrient recovery are lacking. The results of the study indicate that integrated resource recovery can be an environmentally advantageous strategy for dairy manure management, providing an alternative to conventional practices that can better capture nutrients and mitigate GHG emissions directly on the farm. Further research with NEWIR technologies at larger scale will help refine inventory data as well as provide valuable information for on-farm implementation of resource recovery systems.
